pages.title: Algebraic geometry and analytic geometry
pages.html:
XII Algebraic geometry and analytic geometry

Mme M. Raynaud1

1cm

Proceeding as in [10], one associates to every scheme \(X\) locally of finite type over the field of complex numbers \(\mathbf{C}\) an analytic space \(X^\mathrm{an}\) whose underlying set is \(X(\mathbf{C})\).

In 2 and 3 of this exposé, we give a “dictionary” between the usual properties of \(X\) and of \(X^\mathrm{an}\) and between the properties of a morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) and of the associated morphism \(f^\mathrm{an}\colon X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y^\mathrm{an}\). We then show that the comparison theorems between coherent sheaves on \(X\) and \(X^\mathrm{an}\), established in [10] for a projective variety, are still valid when \(X\) is a proper scheme.

Finally, in 5 we prove the equivalence of the category of finite étale coverings of \(X\) and of the category of finite étale coverings of \(X^\mathrm{an}\). As a bonus to the reader, we give a new proof of the theorem of Grauert-Remmert [6], using the resolution of singularities [8].

pages.title: Analytic space associated with a scheme
pages.html:
1 Analytic space associated with a scheme

Let \(X\) be a scheme locally of finite type over \(\mathbf{C}\). Let \(\Phi\) be the functor from the category of analytic spaces [4] to the category of sets, which to an analytic space \(\mathscr{X}\) associates the set of morphisms of ringed spaces in \(\mathbf{C}\)-algebras \(\operatorname{Hom}_\mathbf{C} (\mathscr{X},X)\). We have the following theorem:

Theorem and definition 1

The functor \(\Phi\) is representable by an analytic space \(X^\mathrm{an}\) and a morphism \(\varphi\colon X^\mathrm{an}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). One says that \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is the analytic space associated with \(X\). If \(|X^\mathrm{an}|\) is the underlying set of \(X^\mathrm{an}\), \(\varphi\) induces a bijection of \(|X^\mathrm{an}|\) onto the set \(X(\mathbf{C})\) of points of \(X\) with values in \(\mathbf{C}\). Moreover, for each point \(x\) of \(X^\mathrm{an}\), the morphism

\[ \varphi_x\colon\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi(x)}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_{X^\mathrm{an},x}, \]

which is necessarily local, yields upon passing to the completions an isomorphism

\[ \widehat{\varphi}_x\colon\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X,\varphi(x)}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X^\mathrm{an},x}, \]

In particular the morphism \(\varphi\) is flat.

Let us note that the fact that \(\varphi\) induces a bijection of \(X^\mathrm{an}\) onto \(X(\mathbf{C})\) follows from the universal property of \(X^\mathrm{an}\). On the other hand we have the following assertions:

  1. a)

    If the theorem is true for a scheme \(Y\), it is also so for every subscheme \(X\) of \(Y\). Suppose first that \(X\) is an open subscheme of \(Y\); if \(\psi\colon Y^\mathrm{an}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is the canonical morphism, \(\psi^{-1}(X)\) is an open subset of \(Y^\mathrm{an}\) which one equips with the structure of analytic space induced by that of \(Y^\mathrm{an}\). Since every morphism from an analytic space \(\mathscr{X}\) to \(X\) factors through \(Y^\mathrm{an}\) by the universal property of the latter, hence through \(X^\mathrm{an}\) which is the fibre product \(Y^\mathrm{an}\times_Y X\), \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is the analytic space associated with \(X\). Finally the assertion concerning the \(\varphi_x\) is obvious.

    It now suffices to consider the case where \(X\) is a closed subscheme of \(Y\). Let \(I\) be the coherent \(\mathscr{O}_Y\)-Ideal defining \(X\); then \(I\cdot\mathscr{O}_{Y^\mathrm{an}}\) is a coherent sheaf of ideals on \(\mathscr{O}_{Y^\mathrm{an}}\) which defines a closed analytic subspace \(X^\mathrm{an}\) of \(Y^\mathrm{an}\); one sees as in the case of an open subscheme that \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is the analytic space associated with \(X\). Let \(\varphi\colon X^\mathrm{an}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical morphism. For every point \(x\) of \(X^\mathrm{an}\), the morphism \(\varphi_x\) is none other than the morphism

    \[ \mathscr{O}_{Y,\psi(x)}/I_{\psi(x)}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{Y^\mathrm{an},x}/I_{\psi(x)}\cdot\mathscr{O}_{Y^\mathrm{an},x} \]

    induced by \(\psi_x\); its completion

    \[ \widehat{\varphi}_x\colon \widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{Y,\psi(x)}/I_{\psi(x)}\cdot\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{Y,\psi(x)} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{Y^\mathrm{an},x}/I_{\psi(x)}\cdot\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{Y^\mathrm{an},x} \]

    is an isomorphism since \(\widehat\psi_x\) is one, which proves a).

  2. b)

    If one has two \(\mathbf{C}\)-schemes \(X_1\), \(X_2\), such that \(X_1^\mathrm{an}\) and \(X_2^\mathrm{an}\) exist, then the same holds for \((X_1\times X_2)^\mathrm{an}\). Indeed, let \(\varphi_1\colon X_1^\mathrm{an}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_1\), \(\varphi_2\colon X_2^\mathrm{an}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_2\) be the canonical morphisms, \(p_1\), \(p_2\) the two projections of \(X_1^\mathrm{an}\times X_2^\mathrm{an}\). One deduces formally from EGA I 1.8.1 that \(X_1\times X_2\) is the product of \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) in the category of ringed spaces in local rings; it follows that the morphisms \(\varphi_1\cdot p_1\) and \(\varphi_2\cdot p_2\) define a morphism \(\varphi\colon X_1^\mathrm{an}\times X_2^\mathrm{an}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_1\times X_2\) and that the pair \((X_1^\mathrm{an}\times X_2^\mathrm{an},\varphi)\) represents the functor \(\mathscr{X}\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\operatorname{Hom}_\mathbf{C}(\mathscr{X},X_1\times X_2)\).

  3. c)

    If \(\mathscr{E}^1\) denotes the affine space of dimension 1, i.e. the topological space \(\mathbf{C}\) equipped with the sheaf of holomorphic functions, the functor1 \(\mathscr{X}\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\operatorname{Hom}_\mathbf{C} (\mathscr{X},E^1_\mathbf{C})\) is representable by \(\mathscr{E}^1\), the canonical morphism \(\varphi\colon\mathscr{E}^1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E^1_\mathbf{C}\) being the obvious morphism. Indeed, to give a morphism from an analytic space \(\mathscr{X}\) to \(E^1_\mathbf{C}\) amounts to giving an element of \(\Gamma(\mathscr{X},\mathscr{O}_\mathscr{X})\), which also amounts to giving a morphism from \(\mathscr{X}\) to \(\mathscr{E}^1\). One evidently has a bijection \(|\mathscr{E}^1|\simeq E^1(\mathbf{C})\), and, for each point \(x\in\mathscr{E}^1\), the morphism \(\widehat\varphi_x\) is none other than the identity morphism of a ring of formal power series in one variable over \(\mathbf{C}\).

.5cmOne deduces from b) and c) that the theorem is true for the affine space \(E^n_\mathbf{C}\), \(n\geqslant 0\). Using a), one sees that the same holds for every affine scheme \(X\), locally of finite type over \(\mathbf{C}\). If one no longer supposes \(X\) affine and if \((X_i)\) is a covering of \(X\) by affine open subsets, it follows from the universal property and from a) that the \(X_i^\mathrm{an}\) glue together and thus define the analytic space \(X^\mathrm{an}\) associated with \(X\).

2

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of \(\mathbf{C}\)-schemes locally of finite type. If \(\varphi\colon X^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(\psi\colon Y^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) are the canonical morphisms, it follows from the universal property of \(Y^{\mathrm{an}}\) that there exists a unique morphism \(f^{\mathrm{an}}\colon X^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y^{\mathrm{an}}\) such that the diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\ar[r]\ar[d]_-{f^{\mathrm{an}}}&{X}\ar[d]^-f\\{Y^{\mathrm{an}}}\ar[r]&{Y}} \]

is commutative. One has thus defined a functor \(\Phi\) from the category of \(\mathbf{C}\)-schemes locally of finite type to the category of analytic spaces.

The functor \(\Phi\) commutes with finite inverse limits. Indeed it suffices to see that \(\Phi\) commutes with fibre products. Now, if \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\), are schemes locally of finite type over \(\mathbf{C}\), it follows from the fact that \(X\times_Z Y\) is the fibre product of \(X\) and \(Y\) over \(Z\) in the category of ringed spaces in local rings that \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\times_{Z^{\mathrm{an}}}Y^{\mathrm{an}}\) satisfies the universal property which characterizes \((X\times_Z Y)^{\mathrm{an}}\).

3

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type, \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) the associated analytic space, \(\varphi\colon X^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism. If \(F\) is an \({\mathscr{O}}_X\)-Module, the inverse image \(\varphi^*F=F^{\mathrm{an}}\) is a sheaf of modules on \({\mathscr{O}}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\). One thus defines a functor from the category of \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules to the category of Modules on \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\). This functor commutes with direct limits (EGA 0 4.3.2). Since the sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\) is coherent [4], it transforms coherent sheaves into coherent sheaves (EGA 0 5.3.11). One has moreover:

Proposition 3.1

The functor which to an \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\)-Module \(F\) associates its inverse image \(F^{\mathrm{an}}\) on \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is exact, faithful, conservative.

The exactness follows from the fact that the morphism \(\varphi\colon X^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is flat (1). Let us prove that the functor \(F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} F^{\mathrm{an}}\) is faithful. Taking into account the exactness, it suffices to show that, if \(F^{\mathrm{an}}\) is zero, then so is \(F\). Now, for every point \(x\) of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\), one then has \(F_{\varphi (x)}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi (x)}}\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},x}=0\). Since the morphism \(\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi (x)}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},x}\) is faithfully flat, one has \(F_{\varphi (x)}=0\) for every closed point \(\varphi (x)\) of \(X\), and, since \(X\) is Jacobson (EGA IV 10.4.8), this implies that \(F\) is zero.

The fact that the functor \(F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} F^{\mathrm{an}}\) is conservative is formal from exactness and faithfulness.

pages.title:
Comparison of the properties of a scheme and of the associated
analytic space
pages.html:
2 Comparison of the properties of a scheme and of the associated analytic space

Proposition 1

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type, \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) the associated analytic space, \(n\) an integer. Consider the property \(P\) of being

  • (i)

    non-empty

  • (i')

    discrete

  • (ii)

    Cohen-Macaulay

  • (iii)

    \((\mathrm{S}_n)\)

  • (iv)

    regular

  • (v)

    \((\mathrm{R}_n)\)

  • (vi)

    normal

  • (vii)

    reduced

  • (viii)

    of dimension \(n\).

Then, in order that \(X\) possess the property \(P\), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\).

Let \(\varphi\colon X^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical morphism. (i) follows from the fact that one has \(|X^{\mathrm{an}}|=X(\mathbf{C} )\) (1) and from the fact that \(X\) is Jacobson (EGA IV 10.4.8). To say that \(X\) (resp. \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\)) is discrete amounts to saying that one has \(\dim X=0\) (resp. \(\dim X^{\mathrm{an}}=0\) according to [4]); (i') therefore follows from (viii).

Let \(P\) be one of the properties (ii) to (vii). In order that \(X\) possess the property \(P\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(P\) be satisfied at each closed point of \(X\); indeed, \(X\) being excellent (EGA IV 7.8.6 (iii)), the set of points where \(X\) satisfies \(P\) is an open set (loc. cit. ) and, if this open set contains all the closed points, it is equal to the whole of \(X\). To say that \(X\) (resp. \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\)) has the property \(P\) thus amounts to saying that, for every point \(x\) of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\), the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi (x)}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},x}\)) has the property \(P\). Since the fact that an excellent local ring has the property \(P\) can be seen after passage to the completion, the proposition follows from the isomorphisms \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X,\varphi(x)}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},x}\) in the cases (ii) to (vii). The same holds in the case (viii), taking account of the relations

\[ \dim X=\sup_x~\dim \mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi (x)}\qquad \dim X^{\mathrm{an}}=\sup_x~\dim \mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},x} \]

where \(x\in X^{\mathrm{an}}\). This completes the proof.

Proposition 2

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type, \(\varphi\colon X^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism, \(T\) a locally constructible part of \(X\). Then one has the relation

\[ \varphi^{-1}(\overline{T})=\overline{\varphi^{-1}(T)}. \]

One may suppose that \(T\) is a dense open subset of \(X\). Let \(H\) be the reduced closed subscheme of \(X\) with underlying space \(X-T\); the associated space \(H^{\mathrm{an}}\) is a closed analytic subspace of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) with underlying space \(X^{\mathrm{an}}-\varphi^{-1}(T)\). One must show that every point \(x\) of \(H^{\mathrm{an}}\) belongs to \(\overline{\varphi^{-1}(T)}\). Now, at such a point \(x\), the germ of analytic space \((X^{\mathrm{an}},x)\) contains the sub-germ \((H^{\mathrm{an}},x)\), and the latter is defined by a non-nilpotent Ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},x}\). It then follows from the Nullstellensatz [4] that every open neighbourhood of \(x\) contains points of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) which do not belong to \(H^{\mathrm{an}}\), which indeed proves that one has \(x\in\overline{\varphi^{-1}(T)}\).

Corollary 3

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type, \(\varphi \colon X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism, \(T\) a locally constructible part of \(X\). In order that \(T\) be an open part (resp. a closed part, resp. a dense part), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \(\varphi^{-1} (T)\).

The corollary follows from 2 and from the fact that, \(X\) being a Jacobson scheme (EGA IV 10.4.8), two locally constructible parts of \(X\) which have the same trace on the very dense set \(X (\mathbf{C} )\) are equal.

Proposition 4

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type. In order that \(X\) be connected (resp. irreducible), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \(X^\mathrm{an}\).

Suppose \(X^\mathrm{an} \) connected (resp. irreducible). The image \(X (\mathbf{C}) \) of \(X^\mathrm{an}\) in \(X\) is then connected (resp. irreducible). It follows that \(X\) is connected (resp. irreducible), for the closed parts of \(X\) and \(X (\mathbf{C})\) correspond bijectively (EGA IV 10.1.2).

Conversely suppose \(X\) connected (resp. irreducible), and let us show that the same holds for \(X^\mathrm{an}\). One may restrict oneself to the case where \(X\) is irreducible. Suppose indeed \(X\) connected. Given a point \(x\) of \(X\), the set of points \(y \in X\) such that there exists a finite sequence of irreducible closed subschemes \(X_1, \dots, X_n\) of \(X\), with \(x \in X_1\), \(y \in X_n\), \(X_i \cap X_{i+1} \neq \emptyset \) for \(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n-1\), is a set at once open and closed, hence equal to the whole of \(X\). For a sequence \(X_1, \dots, X_n\) such as above, one also has \(X_{i}^\mathrm{an} \cap X_{i+1}^\mathrm{an} \neq \emptyset \) for \(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n-1 \); if one supposes it proved that the \(X_{i}^\mathrm{an}\) are connected, then the same holds for \(X^\mathrm{an}\).

One now supposes \(X\) irreducible. One may moreover suppose \(X\) affine. Indeed, if \((U_i)_{i \in I}\) is a covering of \(X\) by affine open sets, two of these open sets have a non-empty intersection, and the same property is therefore true for the covering \((U_{i}^\mathrm{an})_{i \in I} \) of \(X^\mathrm{an}\); if one supposes it proved that the \(U_{i}^\mathrm{an} \) are irreducible, then the same holds for \(X^\mathrm{an}\).

One may moreover suppose that \(X\) is normal. Indeed, let \(\widetilde{X}\) be the normalization of \(X\); since the morphism \(\widetilde{X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is surjective, so is \(\widetilde{X}^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^\mathrm{an}\), which proves that, if \(\widetilde{X}^\mathrm{an} \) is irreducible, so is \(X^\mathrm{an}\).

One now supposes \(X\) affine normal. Since the local rings of \(X^\mathrm{an}\) are integral, it comes to the same to say that \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is irreducible or that it is connected. Indeed, if \(\mathscr{F}\) is a closed analytic part of \(X^\mathrm{an}\), the set of points \(x\) of \(X^\mathrm{an}\) where one has \(\operatorname{codim}_x (\mathscr{F}, X^\mathrm{an} ) = 0\) is a closed analytic subset of \(X^\mathrm{an}\) [4] which is also open; if \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is connected, this proves that, if one has \(\mathscr{F} \neq X^\mathrm{an}\), \(\mathscr{F}\) is rare, hence that \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is irreducible. One is thus reduced to showing that \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is connected.

Let

\[ i \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P \]

be a compactification of \(X\), where \(P\) is a projective normal \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme and \(i\) a dominant open immersion. It then follows from [10] that \(P^\mathrm{an}\) is connected. Since \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is obtained by removing from \(P^\mathrm{an}\) a rare closed analytic part, it follows from 5 below that \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is also connected.

Lemma 5

Let \(\mathscr{P} \) be a connected normal analytic space, \(\mathscr{Y}\) a rare closed analytic part; then \(\mathscr{X} = \mathscr{P} - \mathscr{Y}\) is connected.

When \(\mathscr{Y}\) is of codimension \(\geqslant 2\), the proposition follows from [11]. In the general case one may suppose, up to removing from \(\mathscr{P}\) a closed analytic part of codimension \(\geqslant 2\), that \(\mathscr{P}\) and \(\mathscr{Y}\) (considered as a reduced analytic subspace of \(\mathscr{P}\)) are regular. According to the implicit function theorem, every point \(y\) of \(\mathscr{Y}\) possesses a neighbourhood \(\mathscr{U}\) isomorphic to a ball of an affine space \(\mathscr{E}^{n}\), in such a way that \(\mathscr{U} \cap \mathscr{Y}\) is defined by the vanishing of a certain number of coordinate functions. This proves that \(\mathscr{U} - \mathscr{U} \cap \mathscr{Y}\) is connected, and the same therefore holds for \(\mathscr{X}\).

Corollary 6

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type; the morphism

\[ \pi_0 (X^{\mathrm{an}}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0 (X) \]

induced by the canonical morphism \(X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is bijective.

blocks.html: 2 Comparison of the properties of a scheme and of the associated analytic space

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type, \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) the associated analytic space, \(n\) an integer. Consider the property \(P\) of being

  • (i)

    non-empty

  • (i')

    discrete

  • (ii)

    Cohen-Macaulay

  • (iii)

    \((\mathrm{S}_n)\)

  • (iv)

    regular

  • (v)

    \((\mathrm{R}_n)\)

  • (vi)

    normal

  • (vii)

    reduced

  • (viii)

    of dimension \(n\).

Then, in order that \(X\) possess the property \(P\), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\).

Let \(\varphi\colon X^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical morphism. (i) follows from the fact that one has \(|X^{\mathrm{an}}|=X(\mathbf{C} )\) (1) and from the fact that \(X\) is Jacobson (EGA IV 10.4.8). To say that \(X\) (resp. \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\)) is discrete amounts to saying that one has \(\dim X=0\) (resp. \(\dim X^{\mathrm{an}}=0\) according to [4]); (i') therefore follows from (viii).

Let \(P\) be one of the properties (ii) to (vii). In order that \(X\) possess the property \(P\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(P\) be satisfied at each closed point of \(X\); indeed, \(X\) being excellent (EGA IV 7.8.6 (iii)), the set of points where \(X\) satisfies \(P\) is an open set (loc. cit. ) and, if this open set contains all the closed points, it is equal to the whole of \(X\). To say that \(X\) (resp. \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\)) has the property \(P\) thus amounts to saying that, for every point \(x\) of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\), the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi (x)}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},x}\)) has the property \(P\). Since the fact that an excellent local ring has the property \(P\) can be seen after passage to the completion, the proposition follows from the isomorphisms \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X,\varphi(x)}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},x}\) in the cases (ii) to (vii). The same holds in the case (viii), taking account of the relations
\[ \dim X=\sup_x~\dim \mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi (x)}\qquad \dim X^{\mathrm{an}}=\sup_x~\dim \mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},x} \]
where \(x\in X^{\mathrm{an}}\). This completes the proof.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type, \(\varphi\colon X^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism, \(T\) a locally constructible part of \(X\). Then one has the relation

\[ \varphi^{-1}(\overline{T})=\overline{\varphi^{-1}(T)}. \]

One may suppose that \(T\) is a dense open subset of \(X\). Let \(H\) be the reduced closed subscheme of \(X\) with underlying space \(X-T\); the associated space \(H^{\mathrm{an}}\) is a closed analytic subspace of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) with underlying space \(X^{\mathrm{an}}-\varphi^{-1}(T)\). One must show that every point \(x\) of \(H^{\mathrm{an}}\) belongs to \(\overline{\varphi^{-1}(T)}\). Now, at such a point \(x\), the germ of analytic space \((X^{\mathrm{an}},x)\) contains the sub-germ \((H^{\mathrm{an}},x)\), and the latter is defined by a non-nilpotent Ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},x}\). It then follows from the Nullstellensatz [4] that every open neighbourhood of \(x\) contains points of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) which do not belong to \(H^{\mathrm{an}}\), which indeed proves that one has \(x\in\overline{\varphi^{-1}(T)}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type, \(\varphi \colon X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism, \(T\) a locally constructible part of \(X\). In order that \(T\) be an open part (resp. a closed part, resp. a dense part), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \(\varphi^{-1} (T)\).

The corollary follows from 2 and from the fact that, \(X\) being a Jacobson scheme (EGA IV 10.4.8), two locally constructible parts of \(X\) which have the same trace on the very dense set \(X (\mathbf{C} )\) are equal.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type. In order that \(X\) be connected (resp. irreducible), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \(X^\mathrm{an}\).

Suppose \(X^\mathrm{an} \) connected (resp. irreducible). The image \(X (\mathbf{C}) \) of \(X^\mathrm{an}\) in \(X\) is then connected (resp. irreducible). It follows that \(X\) is connected (resp. irreducible), for the closed parts of \(X\) and \(X (\mathbf{C})\) correspond bijectively (EGA IV 10.1.2).

Conversely suppose \(X\) connected (resp. irreducible), and let us show that the same holds for \(X^\mathrm{an}\). One may restrict oneself to the case where \(X\) is irreducible. Suppose indeed \(X\) connected. Given a point \(x\) of \(X\), the set of points \(y \in X\) such that there exists a finite sequence of irreducible closed subschemes \(X_1, \dots, X_n\) of \(X\), with \(x \in X_1\), \(y \in X_n\), \(X_i \cap X_{i+1} \neq \emptyset \) for \(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n-1\), is a set at once open and closed, hence equal to the whole of \(X\). For a sequence \(X_1, \dots, X_n\) such as above, one also has \(X_{i}^\mathrm{an} \cap X_{i+1}^\mathrm{an} \neq \emptyset \) for \(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n-1 \); if one supposes it proved that the \(X_{i}^\mathrm{an}\) are connected, then the same holds for \(X^\mathrm{an}\).

One now supposes \(X\) irreducible. One may moreover suppose \(X\) affine. Indeed, if \((U_i)_{i \in I}\) is a covering of \(X\) by affine open sets, two of these open sets have a non-empty intersection, and the same property is therefore true for the covering \((U_{i}^\mathrm{an})_{i \in I} \) of \(X^\mathrm{an}\); if one supposes it proved that the \(U_{i}^\mathrm{an} \) are irreducible, then the same holds for \(X^\mathrm{an}\).

One may moreover suppose that \(X\) is normal. Indeed, let \(\widetilde{X}\) be the normalization of \(X\); since the morphism \(\widetilde{X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is surjective, so is \(\widetilde{X}^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^\mathrm{an}\), which proves that, if \(\widetilde{X}^\mathrm{an} \) is irreducible, so is \(X^\mathrm{an}\).

One now supposes \(X\) affine normal. Since the local rings of \(X^\mathrm{an}\) are integral, it comes to the same to say that \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is irreducible or that it is connected. Indeed, if \(\mathscr{F}\) is a closed analytic part of \(X^\mathrm{an}\), the set of points \(x\) of \(X^\mathrm{an}\) where one has \(\operatorname{codim}_x (\mathscr{F}, X^\mathrm{an} ) = 0\) is a closed analytic subset of \(X^\mathrm{an}\) [4] which is also open; if \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is connected, this proves that, if one has \(\mathscr{F} \neq X^\mathrm{an}\), \(\mathscr{F}\) is rare, hence that \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is irreducible. One is thus reduced to showing that \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is connected.
Let
\[ i \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P \]

be a compactification of \(X\), where \(P\) is a projective normal \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme and \(i\) a dominant open immersion. It then follows from [10] that \(P^\mathrm{an}\) is connected. Since \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is obtained by removing from \(P^\mathrm{an}\) a rare closed analytic part, it follows from 5 below that \(X^\mathrm{an}\) is also connected.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5

Let \(\mathscr{P} \) be a connected normal analytic space, \(\mathscr{Y}\) a rare closed analytic part; then \(\mathscr{X} = \mathscr{P} - \mathscr{Y}\) is connected.

When \(\mathscr{Y}\) is of codimension \(\geqslant 2\), the proposition follows from [11]. In the general case one may suppose, up to removing from \(\mathscr{P}\) a closed analytic part of codimension \(\geqslant 2\), that \(\mathscr{P}\) and \(\mathscr{Y}\) (considered as a reduced analytic subspace of \(\mathscr{P}\)) are regular. According to the implicit function theorem, every point \(y\) of \(\mathscr{Y}\) possesses a neighbourhood \(\mathscr{U}\) isomorphic to a ball of an affine space \(\mathscr{E}^{n}\), in such a way that \(\mathscr{U} \cap \mathscr{Y}\) is defined by the vanishing of a certain number of coordinate functions. This proves that \(\mathscr{U} - \mathscr{U} \cap \mathscr{Y}\) is connected, and the same therefore holds for \(\mathscr{X}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type; the morphism

\[ \pi_0 (X^{\mathrm{an}}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0 (X) \]

induced by the canonical morphism \(X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is bijective.

pages.title: Comparison of the properties of morphisms
pages.html:
3 Comparison of the properties of morphisms

Proposition 1

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of \(\mathbf{C}\)-schemes locally of finite type, \(f^\mathrm{an} \colon X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y^\mathrm{an}\) the morphism deduced from \(f\) on the associated analytic spaces. Let \(P\) be the property of being

  1. (i)

    flat

  2. (ii)

    unramified (net) (i.e. non-ramified)

  3. (iii)

    étale

  4. (iv)

    smooth

  5. (v)

    normal

  6. (vi)

    reduced

  7. (vii)

    injective

  8. (viii)

    separated

  9. (ix)

    an isomorphism

  10. (x)

    a monomorphism

  11. (xi)

    an open immersion.

Then, in order for \(f\) to possess the property \(P\), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \(f^\mathrm{an}\).

Let us denote by \(\varphi \colon X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \) and \(\psi \colon Y^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) the canonical morphisms. Let \(x\) be a point of \(X^\mathrm{an}\), \(y = f^\mathrm{an} (x)\). The morphisms \(\mathscr{O}_{Y^\mathrm{an}, y} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\mathscr{O}}_{X^\mathrm{an}, x}\) and \(\mathscr{O}_{Y, \psi(y)} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X, \varphi(x)} \) deduced from \(f\) and \(f^{\mathrm{an}}\) give the same morphism upon passage to the completions (1). By [2] (resp. EGA IV 17.4.4) it thus amounts to the same thing to say that \(f^\mathrm{an} \) verifies property (i) (resp. (ii)) or to say that \(f\) verifies (i) (resp. (ii)) at each closed point of \(X\). Since the set of points of \(X\) where (i) (resp. (ii)) is verified is open (EGA IV 11.1.1 and I 3.3), this proves (i) and (ii), hence also (iii).

.5cmLet \(P\) be property (iv) (resp. (v), resp. (vi)). Taking into account 1 ((v), (vi), (vii)), it amounts to the same thing to say that the geometric fibres of \(f^\mathrm{an}\) at the various points \(y\) of \(Y^\mathrm{an}\) are regular (resp. normal, resp. reduced) or that the same holds for the geometric fibres of \(f\) at the various closed points \(\psi (y)\) of \(Y\). The cases (iv) (resp. (v), resp. (vi)) then result from (i) and from the fact that the set of points of \(Y\) where the geometric fibres of \(f\) are regular is open (EGA IV 12.1.7).

(vii). If \(f\) is injective, the same holds for \(f^\mathrm{an}\). Conversely, suppose \(f^\mathrm{an}\) injective and let us show that the same holds for \(f\). We may suppose \(f\) of finite type. The morphism \(f^\mathrm{an}\) being injective, the fibres of \(f\) at the closed points of \(Y\) are radicial; since the set of points of \(Y\) whose fibre is radicial is locally constructible (EGA IV 9.6.1) and since \(Y\) is a Jacobson scheme, \(f\) has all its fibres radicial hence is injective.

(viii). Let \(\Delta \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X \) and \(\Delta^\mathrm{an} \colon X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^\mathrm{an} \times_{Y^\mathrm{an}} X^\mathrm{an} \) be the diagonal immersions, \(\Theta \colon X^\mathrm{an} \times_{Y^\mathrm{an}} X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X \) the canonical morphism. By virtue of 3 it amounts to the same thing to say that \(\Delta (X)\) is closed in \(X \times_Y X \) or that \(\Delta^\mathrm{an} (X^\mathrm{an})\) is closed in \(X^\mathrm{an} \times_{Y^\mathrm{an}} X^\mathrm{an}\).

Since an open immersion is nothing but an étale injective morphism (EGA IV 17.9.1 and [4]), (xi) results from (iii) and from (vii). An isomorphism being the same thing as a surjective open immersion, (ix) results from (xi) and from (2 (i)) below. To say that \(f\) is a monomorphism is equivalent to saying that the diagonal morphism \(\Delta \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X\) is an isomorphism, hence (x) results from (ix).

Proposition 2

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two \(\mathbf{C}\)-schemes locally of finite type, \(f\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a morphism of finite type, \(f^\mathrm{an} \colon X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y^\mathrm{an} \) the morphism deduced from \(f\) on the associated analytic spaces. Let \(P\) be the property of being

  1. (i)

    surjective

  2. (ii)

    dominant

  3. (iii)

    a closed immersion

  4. (iv)

    an immersion

  5. (v)

    proper1

  6. (vi)

    finite.

Then, in order for \(f\) to possess the property \(P\), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \(f^\mathrm{an}\).

Let \(\varphi \colon X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(\psi \colon Y^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be the canonical morphisms.

(i). If \(f\) is surjective, then for every point \(y\) of \(Y^\mathrm{an}\), \(f^{-1} (\psi (y)) \) is a non-empty closed part of \(X\); it therefore contains at least one closed point, which proves that \(f^\mathrm{an}\) is surjective. Conversely, if \(f^\mathrm{an}\) is surjective, \(f(X)\) is a locally constructible part of \(Y\) (EGA IV 1.8.4) which contains all the closed points of \(Y\); one thus has \(f(X)=Y\).

(ii) results from 2.

(iii). If \(f\) is a closed immersion, the same holds for \(f^\mathrm{an}\) by 1 a). Conversely, if \(f^\mathrm{an}\) is a closed immersion, the same holds for \(f\) by 1 (x) and 2 (v), for this amounts to saying that \(f\) is a proper monomorphism (EGA IV 8.11.5).

(iv). It is clear that, if \(f\) is an immersion, the same holds for \(f^\mathrm{an}\). Conversely, suppose that \(f^\mathrm{an}\) is an immersion, and let \(T\) be the image of \(X\) in \(Y\), \(\overline{T}\) the schematic closure of \(f\). One has a factorization of \(f\),

\[ X \xrightarrow{i} \overline{T} \xrightarrow{j} J , \]

where \(j\) is a closed immersion, \(i\) the canonical morphism, and one deduces from it the following factorization of \(f^\mathrm{an}\)

\[ X^\mathrm{an} \xrightarrow{i^\mathrm{an}} \overline{T}^{ \mathrm{an}} \xrightarrow{j^\mathrm{an}} Y^\mathrm{an} \ . \]

Since \(T = f(X)\) is a locally constructible part of \(Y\) (EGA IV 1.8.4), one has, by 2, \(\overline{T}^{ \mathrm{an}} = \overline{f^\mathrm{an} (X^\mathrm{an} )}\). It follows that \(i^\mathrm{an} (X^\mathrm{an} )\) is an open of \(\overline{T}^{ \mathrm{an}}\), hence that \(i(X) \) is an open of \(\overline{T}\). One considers the canonical factorization of \(i\)

\[ X \xrightarrow{i_1} i (X) \xrightarrow{i_2} \overline{T}. \]

The morphism \(i_1^\mathrm{an} \) is a proper monomorphism, hence the same holds for \(i_1\) by 2 (v) and 1 (x); this proves that \(i_1\) hence also \(f\) is an immersion.

(v). Suppose that \(f\) is proper and let us show that the same holds for \(f^\mathrm{an}\). The fact that \(f^\mathrm{an} \) is proper being local on \(Y^\mathrm{an}\), one may suppose \(Y\) affine. By Chow's lemma (EGA II 5.6.1), one can find a projective \(Y\)-scheme \(X'\) and a surjective projective morphism

\[ g \colon X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X. \]

The morphism \((fg)^\mathrm{an} = f^\mathrm{an} g^\mathrm{an} \) is projective hence proper, \(g^\mathrm{an} \) is surjective, and it results from [1] that \(f^\mathrm{an} \) is proper.

Conversely, suppose \(f^\mathrm{an} \) proper and let us show that the same holds for \(f\). By 1 (viii) \(f\) is separated. It remains to prove that \(f\) is universally closed, and it even suffices to show that \(f\) is closed; indeed, for every \(Y\)-scheme \(Y'\) locally of finite type, the morphism

\[ f_{(Y')} = h \colon X \times_Y Y' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y' \]

will also be closed since \(h^\mathrm{an} \) is proper. Let \(T\) be a closed part of \(X\); \(f(T)\) is a locally constructible set, and one has

\[ f^\mathrm{an} (\varphi^{-1} (T)) = \psi^{-1} (f(T)). \]

Since \(f^\mathrm{an} \) is proper, \(\psi^{-1} (f (T))\) is a closed part of \(Y^\mathrm{an} \), and it therefore results from 2 that one has

\[ \psi^{-1} (\overline{f(T)}) = \psi^{-1} (f(T)) . \]

This entails that one has \(f(T) = \overline{f(T)}\), i.e. that \(f\) is closed hence that \(f\) is proper.

(vi). It amounts to the same thing to say that a morphism is finite or that it is proper with finite fibres (EGA III 4.4.2 and [4]). Since the set of points where the fibres of \(f\) are finite is locally constructible (EGA IV 9.7.9), the fibres of \(f\) are finite if and only if the same holds for the fibres of \(f^\mathrm{an} \); (vi) thus follows from (v).

Remark 3

  1. a)

    let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of \(\mathbf{C}\)-schemes locally of finite type. The fact that \(f^\mathrm{an} \) is a local isomorphism does not imply that the same holds for \(f\). Indeed, if \(f\) is étale, \(f^\mathrm{an} \) is étale hence is a local isomorphism [4], but this is not necessarily the case for \(f\).

  2. b)

    The statement 2 is not true if one does not assume \(f\) to be of finite type. Let us show for example that \(f^\mathrm{an} \) may be a closed immersion without the same being true of \(f\). It suffices indeed to take for \(X\) the sum of \(\mathbf{Z} \) copies of \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbf{C}\), for \(Y\) the affine line, and for \(f\) the morphism obtained by sending the points of \(X\) to distinct points of \(Y\) forming a discrete subset.

(viii). Let \(\Delta \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X \) and \(\Delta^\mathrm{an} \colon X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^\mathrm{an} \times_{Y^\mathrm{an}} X^\mathrm{an} \) be the diagonal immersions, \(\Theta \colon X^\mathrm{an} \times_{Y^\mathrm{an}} X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X \) the canonical morphism. By virtue of 3 it amounts to the same thing to say that \(\Delta (X)\) is closed in \(X \times_Y X \) or that \(\Delta^\mathrm{an} (X^\mathrm{an})\) is closed in \(X^\mathrm{an} \times_{Y^\mathrm{an}} X^\mathrm{an}\).

Since an open immersion is nothing but an étale injective morphism (EGA IV 17.9.1 and [4]), (xi) results from (iii) and from (vii). An isomorphism being the same thing as a surjective open immersion, (ix) results from (xi) and from (2 (i)) below. To say that \(f\) is a monomorphism is equivalent to saying that the diagonal morphism \(\Delta \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X\) is an isomorphism, hence (x) results from (ix).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two \(\mathbf{C}\)-schemes locally of finite type, \(f\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a morphism of finite type, \(f^\mathrm{an} \colon X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y^\mathrm{an} \) the morphism deduced from \(f\) on the associated analytic spaces. Let \(P\) be the property of being

  1. (i)

    surjective

  2. (ii)

    dominant

  3. (iii)

    a closed immersion

  4. (iv)

    an immersion

  5. (v)

    proper1

  6. (vi)

    finite.

Then, in order for \(f\) to possess the property \(P\), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \(f^\mathrm{an}\).

Let \(\varphi \colon X^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(\psi \colon Y^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be the canonical morphisms.

(i). If \(f\) is surjective, then for every point \(y\) of \(Y^\mathrm{an}\), \(f^{-1} (\psi (y)) \) is a non-empty closed part of \(X\); it therefore contains at least one closed point, which proves that \(f^\mathrm{an}\) is surjective. Conversely, if \(f^\mathrm{an}\) is surjective, \(f(X)\) is a locally constructible part of \(Y\) (EGA IV 1.8.4) which contains all the closed points of \(Y\); one thus has \(f(X)=Y\).
(ii) results from 2.

(iii). If \(f\) is a closed immersion, the same holds for \(f^\mathrm{an}\) by 1 a). Conversely, if \(f^\mathrm{an}\) is a closed immersion, the same holds for \(f\) by 1 (x) and 2 (v), for this amounts to saying that \(f\) is a proper monomorphism (EGA IV 8.11.5).

(iv). It is clear that, if \(f\) is an immersion, the same holds for \(f^\mathrm{an}\). Conversely, suppose that \(f^\mathrm{an}\) is an immersion, and let \(T\) be the image of \(X\) in \(Y\), \(\overline{T}\) the schematic closure of \(f\). One has a factorization of \(f\),
\[ X \xrightarrow{i} \overline{T} \xrightarrow{j} J , \]
where \(j\) is a closed immersion, \(i\) the canonical morphism, and one deduces from it the following factorization of \(f^\mathrm{an}\)
\[ X^\mathrm{an} \xrightarrow{i^\mathrm{an}} \overline{T}^{ \mathrm{an}} \xrightarrow{j^\mathrm{an}} Y^\mathrm{an} \ . \]

Since \(T = f(X)\) is a locally constructible part of \(Y\) (EGA IV 1.8.4), one has, by 2, \(\overline{T}^{ \mathrm{an}} = \overline{f^\mathrm{an} (X^\mathrm{an} )}\). It follows that \(i^\mathrm{an} (X^\mathrm{an} )\) is an open of \(\overline{T}^{ \mathrm{an}}\), hence that \(i(X) \) is an open of \(\overline{T}\). One considers the canonical factorization of \(i\)
\[ X \xrightarrow{i_1} i (X) \xrightarrow{i_2} \overline{T}. \]
The morphism \(i_1^\mathrm{an} \) is a proper monomorphism, hence the same holds for \(i_1\) by 2 (v) and 1 (x); this proves that \(i_1\) hence also \(f\) is an immersion.

(v). Suppose that \(f\) is proper and let us show that the same holds for \(f^\mathrm{an}\). The fact that \(f^\mathrm{an} \) is proper being local on \(Y^\mathrm{an}\), one may suppose \(Y\) affine. By Chow's lemma (EGA II 5.6.1), one can find a projective \(Y\)-scheme \(X'\) and a surjective projective morphism
\[ g \colon X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X. \]
The morphism \((fg)^\mathrm{an} = f^\mathrm{an} g^\mathrm{an} \) is projective hence proper, \(g^\mathrm{an} \) is surjective, and it results from [1] that \(f^\mathrm{an} \) is proper.

Conversely, suppose \(f^\mathrm{an} \) proper and let us show that the same holds for \(f\). By 1 (viii) \(f\) is separated. It remains to prove that \(f\) is universally closed, and it even suffices to show that \(f\) is closed; indeed, for every \(Y\)-scheme \(Y'\) locally of finite type, the morphism
\[ f_{(Y')} = h \colon X \times_Y Y' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y' \]
will also be closed since \(h^\mathrm{an} \) is proper. Let \(T\) be a closed part of \(X\); \(f(T)\) is a locally constructible set, and one has
\[ f^\mathrm{an} (\varphi^{-1} (T)) = \psi^{-1} (f(T)). \]
Since \(f^\mathrm{an} \) is proper, \(\psi^{-1} (f (T))\) is a closed part of \(Y^\mathrm{an} \), and it therefore results from 2 that one has
\[ \psi^{-1} (\overline{f(T)}) = \psi^{-1} (f(T)) . \]
This entails that one has \(f(T) = \overline{f(T)}\), i.e. that \(f\) is closed hence that \(f\) is proper.

(vi). It amounts to the same thing to say that a morphism is finite or that it is proper with finite fibres (EGA III 4.4.2 and [4]). Since the set of points where the fibres of \(f\) are finite is locally constructible (EGA IV 9.7.9), the fibres of \(f\) are finite if and only if the same holds for the fibres of \(f^\mathrm{an} \); (vi) thus follows from (v).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3

  1. a)

    let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of \(\mathbf{C}\)-schemes locally of finite type. The fact that \(f^\mathrm{an} \) is a local isomorphism does not imply that the same holds for \(f\). Indeed, if \(f\) is étale, \(f^\mathrm{an} \) is étale hence is a local isomorphism [4], but this is not necessarily the case for \(f\).

  2. b)

    The statement 2 is not true if one does not assume \(f\) to be of finite type. Let us show for example that \(f^\mathrm{an} \) may be a closed immersion without the same being true of \(f\). It suffices indeed to take for \(X\) the sum of \(\mathbf{Z} \) copies of \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbf{C}\), for \(Y\) the affine line, and for \(f\) the morphism obtained by sending the points of \(X\) to distinct points of \(Y\) forming a discrete subset.

footnotes.html: We shall say that a morphism of analytic spaces is proper if it is so in the sense of [1] and if it is separated.

pages.title: Cohomological comparison theorems and existence theorems
pages.html:
4 Cohomological comparison theorems and existence theorems

The purpose of this article is to reprove the results of [3]; the latter generalize to the case of a proper scheme the theorems established in [10] when \(X\) is projective, and extend them to the relative case. More general results, concerning the proper relative schemes over an analytic space, are proved in [7].

Let us recall that the Čech cohomology used in [10] coincides with the usual cohomology in the algebraic case as in the analytic case (EGA III 1.4.1. and [5]).

1

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y \) be a morphism of \(\mathbf{C}\)-schemes locally of finite type and consider the commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{X^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]^\varphi\ar[d]_{f^\mathrm{an}}&{X}\ar[d]^f\\{Y^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]^\psi&{Y .}} \]

If \(F\) is an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module, one has, for every integer \(p \geqslant 0\), morphisms

\[ \mathrm{R}^p f_* F \xrightarrow{i} \mathrm{R}^p f_* (\varphi_* F^\mathrm{an} ) \xrightarrow{j} \mathrm{R}^p (f\cdot\varphi)_* F^\mathrm{an} \xrightarrow{k} \psi_* (\mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} F^\mathrm{an} ) , \]

where \(i\) is deduced from the canonical morphism \(F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varphi_* F^\mathrm{an} \), and \(j, k\) are “edge-homomorphisms” of Leray spectral sequences. To the composite \(k.j.i\) is associated a canonical morphism

\[ \tag{4.1.1} {\theta_p \colon (\mathrm{R}^p f_* F)^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} (F^\mathrm{an} )} \]
Theorem 2

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y \) be a proper morphism of \(\mathbf{C}\)-schemes locally of finite type, \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Then, for every integer \(p \geqslant 0\) the morphism (4.1.1)

\[ \theta_p \colon (\mathrm{R}^p f_* F)^\mathrm{an} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} (F^\mathrm{an}) \]

is an isomorphism.

1) Case where \(f\) is projective. The proof is analogous to that of [10]. Let us recall it briefly. One reduces to the case where \(X\) is a projective space of type \(\mathbf{P}^r_Y \) over \(Y\). Let \(\mathscr{Y} = Y^\mathrm{an}\), \(\mathscr{P} = \mathbf{P}^r_\mathscr{Y}\); one first proves that one has

\[ f_*^\mathrm{an} \mathscr{O}_\mathscr{P} = \mathscr{O}_\mathscr{Y} , \qquad \mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} (\mathscr{O}_\mathscr{P}) = 0 \qquad \text{for } p>0 \]

To verify the preceding relations, one may indeed reduce to the case where \(\mathscr{Y}\) is a ball \(\mathscr{B}\) of an affine space \(\mathscr{E}^n\). One considers the “standard covering” \(\{ \mathscr{U}_i \}\) of \(\mathscr{P}\) by \(r+1\) open sets isomorphic to \(\mathscr{B} \times \mathscr{E}^r\). Since these open sets are of Stein type, one has, for every integer \(p \geqslant 0\), isomorphisms

\[ \mathrm{H}^p (\{ \mathscr{U}_i \}, \mathscr{O}_\mathscr{P}) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{H}^p (\mathscr{P}, \mathscr{O}_\mathscr{P}) \]

One may then express the sections of the structural sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_\mathscr{P}\) over the open sets \(\mathscr{U}_i \) and over their intersections in terms of Laurent series; an easy computation proves that one has

\[ \mathrm{H}^0 (\mathscr{P}, \mathscr{O}_\mathscr{P} ) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0 (\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{O}_\mathscr{Y} ) , \qquad \mathrm{H}^p (\mathscr{P}, \mathscr{O}_\mathscr{P} ) = 0 \qquad \text{for } p>0 \]

The proof is then completed by copying [10], the cohomology groups being replaced by the cohomology sheaves.

2) Case where \(f\) is proper. One uses EGA III 3.1.2 to reduce to the projective case. Let \(\mathscr{K}\) be the category of the coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules such that \(\theta_p\) is an isomorphism for every \(p \geqslant 0\). It suffices to prove that, for every exact sequence \(0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) two of whose terms are in \(\mathscr{K}\), the same holds for the third, that a direct factor of an object of \(\mathscr{K}\) is in \(\mathscr{K}\), and that, for every point \(x\) of \(X\), one can find an object \(F\) of \(\mathscr{K}\) such that one has \(F_x \neq 0\).

The first condition results from applying the five lemma to the following commutative diagram whose rows are exact:

\[ \xymatrix@=.7cm{ \ar[r] &{(\mathrm{R}^p f_* F')^\mathrm{an}}\ar[d]\ar[r]&{(\mathrm{R}^p f_* F)^\mathrm{an}}\ar[d]\ar[r]&{(\mathrm{R}^p f_* F'')^\mathrm{an}}\ar[d]\ar[r]&{(\mathrm{R}^{p+1} f_* F' )^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\,}\\ \ar[r] &{\mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} F'^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} F^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} F''^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{R}^{p+1} f_*^\mathrm{an} F'^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]&{,}} \]

and one verifies the second condition in an analogous manner.

To verify the third condition, one may restrict oneself to the case where \(X\) is an irreducible scheme with generic point \(x\). One could suppose \(Y\) Noetherian from the outset. By Chow's lemma (EGA II 5.6.1), one can find a projective \(Y\)-scheme \(X'\) and a projective surjective morphism \(g \colon X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). On the other hand there exists an integer \(n\) such that one has \(\mathrm{R}^p g_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)) = 0 \) for every \(p>0\) and such that the canonical morphism \(g^* g_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)\) is surjective (EGA III 2.2.1). If one sets \(F = g_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)) \), the sheaf \(F\) answers the question. Indeed one has \(F_x \neq 0\); moreover the Leray spectral sequence

\[ \mathrm{R}^p f_* (\mathrm{R}^q g_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n))) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^{p+q} (f\cdot g)_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)) \]

being degenerate, one has an isomorphism

\[ \mathrm{R}^p f_* F \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{R}^p (f\cdot g)_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)) \]

As in the algebraic case one has a canonical isomorphism

\[ \mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} F^\mathrm{an} \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{R}^p (f\cdot g)_*^\mathrm{an} (\mathscr{O}_{X'}(n)^\mathrm{an} ), \]

and the diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=1cm{{(\mathrm{R}^p f_* F)^\mathrm{an}}\ar[d]_{\theta_p}\ar[r]^-{\sim}&{(\mathrm{R}^p (f\cdot g)_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)))^\mathrm{an}}\ar[d]_{\psi_p}\\{\mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} F^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]^-{\sim}&{\mathrm{R}^p (f\cdot g)_*^\mathrm{an} (\mathscr{O}_{X'}(n)^\mathrm{an})}} \]

is commutative. By 1) \(\psi_p \) is an isomorphism; the same therefore holds for \(\theta_p\), which completes the proof.

Corollary 3

Let \(X\) be a proper \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme, \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Then, for every integer \(p\geqslant 0\), the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^p(X,F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^p(X^{\mathrm{an}},F^{\mathrm{an}}) \]

is an isomorphism.

Theorem 4

Let \(X\) be a proper \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme. The functor which, to every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\), associates its inverse image \(F^{\mathrm{an}}\) on \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is an equivalence of categories.

1) The functor is fully faithful. Let indeed \(F\) and \(G\) be two coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules. The canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F,G)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}}(F^{\mathrm{an}},G^{\mathrm{an}}) \]

is identified with the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(X,\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F,G))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(X^{\mathrm{an}},\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F,G)) \]

(EGA \(0_{\textup{I}}\) 6.7.6). Since \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F,G)\) is coherent, it follows from 3 that this morphism is bijective.

2) The functor is essentially surjective. When \(X\) is projective the assertion follows from [10]. The general case reduces to the preceding one by using Chow's lemma (EGA II 5.6.1). Indeed, let \(X'\) be a projective \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme, \(f\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) a surjective projective morphism, \(U\) a dense open subset of \(X\) such that \(f\) induces an isomorphism \(f^{-1}(U)\simeq U\). One argues by Noetherian induction on \(X\); one may therefore suppose that, for every coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{G}\) on \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) such that one can find a closed subset \(Y\) of \(X\) distinct from \(X\), satisfying the relation \(Y^{\mathrm{an}}\supset \operatorname{Supp} \mathscr{G}\), there exists a coherent sheaf \(G\) on \(X\) such that one has an isomorphism \(G^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{G}\).

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a coherent sheaf of modules on \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\), \(\mathscr{K}\) and \(\mathscr{L}\) the coherent sheaves defined by the condition that the sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{K}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_*^{\mathrm{an}}f^{\mathrm{an} *}\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{L}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

be exact. Since \(X'\) is projective, there exists a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\)-Module \(F'\) such that one has \(F'^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq {f^{\mathrm{an}}}^*\mathscr{F}\); one then deduces from 2 that one has an isomorphism \((f_*F')^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq f_*^{\mathrm{an}}{f^{\mathrm{an}}}^*\mathscr{F}\). Since \(\mathscr{K}|U^{\mathrm{an}}\) and \(\mathscr{L}|U^{\mathrm{an}}\) are zero, there exist coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules \(K\) and \(L\) such that one has isomorphisms \(K^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{K}\), \(L^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{L}\). By 1) the morphism \(f_*^{\mathrm{an}}{f^{\mathrm{an}}}^*\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{L}\) comes from a unique morphism \(f_*F'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L\); let \(I=\operatorname{Ker}(f_*F'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L)\). The sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) is then an extension of \(I^{\mathrm{an}}\) by \(K^{\mathrm{an}}\), and it suffices to see that this extension comes by inverse image from an extension of \(I\) by \(K\). It therefore suffices to prove that the canonical morphism

\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(I,K)^{\mathrm{an}}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}}^q(I^{\mathrm{an}},K^{\mathrm{an}})\quad q\neq 1 \]

is bijective. Now one has isomorphisms \(\mathbf{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(I,K)^{\mathrm{an}}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbf{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}}^q(I^{\mathrm{an}},K^{\mathrm{an}})\) for every integer \(q\geqslant 0\) (EGA \(0_{\textup{III}}\) 12.3.5), and a morphism of spectral sequences

\[ \xymatrix@R=.7cm@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{H}^p(X,\mathbf{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(I,K))}\ar@{=>}[r]\ar[d]&{\mathrm{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^{p+q}(I,K)}\ar[d]\\{\mathrm{H}^p(X^{\mathrm{an}},\mathbf{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}}^q(I^{\mathrm{an}},K^{\mathrm{an}}))}\ar@{=>}[r]&{\mathrm{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}}^{p+q}(I^{\mathrm{an}},K^{\mathrm{an}}).}} \]

This morphism is an isomorphism because, by 3, it is so on the terms \(E^{pq}_2\), and this proves the bijectivity of \((*)\).

Corollary 5

The functor which to every proper \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme \(X\) associates \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is fully faithful.

One must show that, if \(X\) and \(Y\) are two proper \(\mathbf{C}\)-schemes, the canonical map

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbf{C}}(X,Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(X^{\mathrm{an}},Y^{\mathrm{an}}) \]

is bijective. Now to give a morphism from \(X\) to \(Y\) (resp. from \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) to \(Y^{\mathrm{an}}\)) amounts to giving its graph, i.e. a closed subscheme \(Z\) of \(X\times Y\) (resp. a closed analytic subspace \(\mathscr{Z}\) of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\times Y^{\mathrm{an}}\)), such that the restriction of the first projection \(X\times Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) to \(Z\) (resp. of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\times Y^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^{\mathrm{an}}\) to \(\mathscr{Z}\)) is an isomorphism. Since the datum of a closed subscheme of \(X\times Y\) (resp. of a closed analytic subspace of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\times Y^{\mathrm{an}}\)) is equivalent to that of a coherent sheaf of ideals on \(\mathscr{O}_{X\times Y}\) (resp. on \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}\times Y^{\mathrm{an}}}\)), the corollary follows from 4.

Corollary 6

Let \(X\) be a proper \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme. The functor which, to every finite (resp. finite étale) scheme \(X'\) over \(X\), associates \(X'^{\mathrm{an}}\) is an equivalence of the category of finite (resp. finite étale) schemes over \(X\) into the category of finite (resp. finite étale) analytic spaces over \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\).

Indeed, to give a finite morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) (resp. \(X'^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^{\mathrm{an}}\)) amounts to giving a coherent sheaf of algebras on \(\mathscr{O}_X\) (resp. on \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\)) [4]. The corollary therefore follows from 4 in the non-étale case, and the étale case is deduced from it, taking into account 1 (iii).

Theorem 2
