Let \(\mathscr{K}\) be the category of the coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules such that \(\theta_p\) is an isomorphism for every \(p \geqslant 0\). It suffices to prove that, for every exact sequence \(0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) two of whose terms are in \(\mathscr{K}\), the same holds for the third, that a direct factor of an object of \(\mathscr{K}\) is in \(\mathscr{K}\), and that, for every point \(x\) of \(X\), one can find an object \(F\) of \(\mathscr{K}\) such that one has \(F_x \neq 0\).
The first condition results from applying the five lemma to the following commutative diagram whose rows are exact:

\[ \xymatrix@=.7cm{ \ar[r] &{(\mathrm{R}^p f_* F')^\mathrm{an}}\ar[d]\ar[r]&{(\mathrm{R}^p f_* F)^\mathrm{an}}\ar[d]\ar[r]&{(\mathrm{R}^p f_* F'')^\mathrm{an}}\ar[d]\ar[r]&{(\mathrm{R}^{p+1} f_* F' )^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\,}\\ \ar[r] &{\mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} F'^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} F^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} F''^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{R}^{p+1} f_*^\mathrm{an} F'^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]&{,}} \]
and one verifies the second condition in an analogous manner.

To verify the third condition, one may restrict oneself to the case where \(X\) is an irreducible scheme with generic point \(x\). One could suppose \(Y\) Noetherian from the outset. By Chow's lemma (EGA II 5.6.1), one can find a projective \(Y\)-scheme \(X'\) and a projective surjective morphism \(g \colon X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). On the other hand there exists an integer \(n\) such that one has \(\mathrm{R}^p g_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)) = 0 \) for every \(p>0\) and such that the canonical morphism \(g^* g_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)\) is surjective (EGA III 2.2.1). If one sets \(F = g_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)) \), the sheaf \(F\) answers the question. Indeed one has \(F_x \neq 0\); moreover the Leray spectral sequence
\[ \mathrm{R}^p f_* (\mathrm{R}^q g_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n))) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^{p+q} (f\cdot g)_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)) \]
being degenerate, one has an isomorphism
\[ \mathrm{R}^p f_* F \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{R}^p (f\cdot g)_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)) \]
As in the algebraic case one has a canonical isomorphism
\[ \mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} F^\mathrm{an} \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{R}^p (f\cdot g)_*^\mathrm{an} (\mathscr{O}_{X'}(n)^\mathrm{an} ), \]
and the diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=1cm{{(\mathrm{R}^p f_* F)^\mathrm{an}}\ar[d]_{\theta_p}\ar[r]^-{\sim}&{(\mathrm{R}^p (f\cdot g)_* (\mathscr{O}_{X'} (n)))^\mathrm{an}}\ar[d]_{\psi_p}\\{\mathrm{R}^p f_*^\mathrm{an} F^\mathrm{an}}\ar[r]^-{\sim}&{\mathrm{R}^p (f\cdot g)_*^\mathrm{an} (\mathscr{O}_{X'}(n)^\mathrm{an})}} \]
is commutative. By 1) \(\psi_p \) is an isomorphism; the same therefore holds for \(\theta_p\), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Let \(X\) be a proper \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme, \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Then, for every integer \(p\geqslant 0\), the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^p(X,F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^p(X^{\mathrm{an}},F^{\mathrm{an}}) \]

is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4

Let \(X\) be a proper \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme. The functor which, to every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\), associates its inverse image \(F^{\mathrm{an}}\) on \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is an equivalence of categories.
1) The functor is fully faithful. Let indeed \(F\) and \(G\) be two coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules. The canonical morphism
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F,G)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}}(F^{\mathrm{an}},G^{\mathrm{an}}) \]
is identified with the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(X,\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F,G))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(X^{\mathrm{an}},\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F,G)) \]
(EGA \(0_{\textup{I}}\) 6.7.6). Since \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F,G)\) is coherent, it follows from 3 that this morphism is bijective.

2) The functor is essentially surjective. When \(X\) is projective the assertion follows from [10]. The general case reduces to the preceding one by using Chow's lemma (EGA II 5.6.1). Indeed, let \(X'\) be a projective \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme, \(f\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) a surjective projective morphism, \(U\) a dense open subset of \(X\) such that \(f\) induces an isomorphism \(f^{-1}(U)\simeq U\). One argues by Noetherian induction on \(X\); one may therefore suppose that, for every coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{G}\) on \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) such that one can find a closed subset \(Y\) of \(X\) distinct from \(X\), satisfying the relation \(Y^{\mathrm{an}}\supset \operatorname{Supp} \mathscr{G}\), there exists a coherent sheaf \(G\) on \(X\) such that one has an isomorphism \(G^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{G}\).
Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a coherent sheaf of modules on \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\), \(\mathscr{K}\) and \(\mathscr{L}\) the coherent sheaves defined by the condition that the sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{K}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_*^{\mathrm{an}}f^{\mathrm{an} *}\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{L}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

be exact. Since \(X'\) is projective, there exists a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\)-Module \(F'\) such that one has \(F'^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq {f^{\mathrm{an}}}^*\mathscr{F}\); one then deduces from 2 that one has an isomorphism \((f_*F')^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq f_*^{\mathrm{an}}{f^{\mathrm{an}}}^*\mathscr{F}\). Since \(\mathscr{K}|U^{\mathrm{an}}\) and \(\mathscr{L}|U^{\mathrm{an}}\) are zero, there exist coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules \(K\) and \(L\) such that one has isomorphisms \(K^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{K}\), \(L^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{L}\). By 1) the morphism \(f_*^{\mathrm{an}}{f^{\mathrm{an}}}^*\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{L}\) comes from a unique morphism \(f_*F'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L\); let \(I=\operatorname{Ker}(f_*F'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L)\). The sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) is then an extension of \(I^{\mathrm{an}}\) by \(K^{\mathrm{an}}\), and it suffices to see that this extension comes by inverse image from an extension of \(I\) by \(K\). It therefore suffices to prove that the canonical morphism
\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(I,K)^{\mathrm{an}}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}}^q(I^{\mathrm{an}},K^{\mathrm{an}})\quad q\neq 1 \]

is bijective. Now one has isomorphisms \(\mathbf{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(I,K)^{\mathrm{an}}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbf{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}}^q(I^{\mathrm{an}},K^{\mathrm{an}})\) for every integer \(q\geqslant 0\) (EGA \(0_{\textup{III}}\) 12.3.5), and a morphism of spectral sequences

\[ \xymatrix@R=.7cm@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{H}^p(X,\mathbf{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(I,K))}\ar@{=>}[r]\ar[d]&{\mathrm{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^{p+q}(I,K)}\ar[d]\\{\mathrm{H}^p(X^{\mathrm{an}},\mathbf{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}}^q(I^{\mathrm{an}},K^{\mathrm{an}}))}\ar@{=>}[r]&{\mathrm{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}}^{p+q}(I^{\mathrm{an}},K^{\mathrm{an}}).}} \]
This morphism is an isomorphism because, by 3, it is so on the terms \(E^{pq}_2\), and this proves the bijectivity of \((*)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

The functor which to every proper \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme \(X\) associates \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is fully faithful.
One must show that, if \(X\) and \(Y\) are two proper \(\mathbf{C}\)-schemes, the canonical map
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbf{C}}(X,Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(X^{\mathrm{an}},Y^{\mathrm{an}}) \]

is bijective. Now to give a morphism from \(X\) to \(Y\) (resp. from \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) to \(Y^{\mathrm{an}}\)) amounts to giving its graph, i.e. a closed subscheme \(Z\) of \(X\times Y\) (resp. a closed analytic subspace \(\mathscr{Z}\) of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\times Y^{\mathrm{an}}\)), such that the restriction of the first projection \(X\times Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) to \(Z\) (resp. of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\times Y^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^{\mathrm{an}}\) to \(\mathscr{Z}\)) is an isomorphism. Since the datum of a closed subscheme of \(X\times Y\) (resp. of a closed analytic subspace of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\times Y^{\mathrm{an}}\)) is equivalent to that of a coherent sheaf of ideals on \(\mathscr{O}_{X\times Y}\) (resp. on \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}\times Y^{\mathrm{an}}}\)), the corollary follows from 4.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6

Let \(X\) be a proper \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme. The functor which, to every finite (resp. finite étale) scheme \(X'\) over \(X\), associates \(X'^{\mathrm{an}}\) is an equivalence of the category of finite (resp. finite étale) schemes over \(X\) into the category of finite (resp. finite étale) analytic spaces over \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\).

Indeed, to give a finite morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) (resp. \(X'^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^{\mathrm{an}}\)) amounts to giving a coherent sheaf of algebras on \(\mathscr{O}_X\) (resp. on \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\)) [4]. The corollary therefore follows from 4 in the non-étale case, and the étale case is deduced from it, taking into account 1 (iii).

pages.title: Comparison theorems for étale coverings
pages.html:
5 Comparison theorems for étale coverings

0

Let us make precise the notion of a finite covering of an analytic space. If \(\mathscr{X}\) is an analytic space, one says that an analytic space \(\mathscr{X}'\) finite over \(\mathscr{X}\) is a finite covering of \(\mathscr{X}\) if every irreducible component of \(\mathscr{X}'\) dominates an irreducible component of \(\mathscr{X}\).

Theorem 1 (“Riemann Existence Theorem”)

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type, \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) the analytic space associated with \(X\). The functor \(\Psi\) which, to every finite étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\), associates \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\) is an equivalence of the category of finite étale coverings of \(X\) into the category of finite étale coverings of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\).

1) The functor \(\Psi\) is fully faithful. Let \(X'\) and \(X''\) be two finite étale coverings of \(X\), and let us prove that the canonical map

\[ \tag{*} { \operatorname{Hom}_{X}(X',X'') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}},X^{\prime\prime\mathrm{an}}) } \]

is bijective. One may assume \(X'\) connected. To give a \(X\)-morphism of \(X'\) into \(X''\) amounts to giving a connected component \(X_{i}\) of \(X'\times_{X}X''\) such that the morphism \(X_{i}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) induced by the first projection is an isomorphism. Since the connected components of \(X'\times_{X}X''\) correspond bijectively to the connected components of \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\times_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}X^{\prime\prime\mathrm{an}}\) (6) and since a morphism \(X_{i}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) is an isomorphism if and only if the same holds for \(X_{i}^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\), this proves the bijectivity of (*).

2) The functor \(\Psi\) is essentially surjective. Let \(\mathscr{X}'\) be a finite étale covering of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) and let us prove that there exists an étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\) such that one has an isomorphism \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathscr{X}'\). By 1) the question is local on \(X\), and one may therefore assume \(X\) affine.

a) Reduction to the case where \(X\) is normal. One may assume \(X\) reduced. Indeed, suppose the theorem proved for \(X_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\). The functor which, to a finite étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\) associates the finite étale covering \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) of \(X_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}^{\mathrm{an}}\) is then an equivalence. Since it is obtained by composing \(\Psi\) with the functor \(\Theta\) which, to a finite étale covering of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) associates its inverse image on \(X_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}^{\mathrm{an}}\), and since \(\Theta\) is fully faithful, this shows that \(\Psi\) is an equivalence of categories.

One may assume \(X\) normal. Indeed, let \(\widetilde{X}\) be the normalization of \(X\), \(p\colon\widetilde{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism. Since \(p\) is finite, \(p\) is a morphism of effective descent for the category of étale coverings (IX 7). The theorem being assumed proved for \(\widetilde{X}\), if one sets \(\widetilde{\mathscr{X}'}=\mathscr{X}'\times_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\widetilde{X}^{\mathrm{an}}\), there exists an étale covering \(\widetilde{X}'\) of \(\widetilde{X}\) and an isomorphism \(\widetilde{X}'{}^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq \widetilde{\mathscr{X}'}\). It then follows from 1) that the descent datum naturally borne by \(\widetilde{\mathscr{X}'}\) lifts to a descent datum on \(\widetilde{X}'\) relative to \(\widetilde{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\); this proves the existence of an étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\) such that one has an isomorphism \(i\colon X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\times_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\widetilde{X}^{\mathrm{an}} \simeq \widetilde{\mathscr{X}'}\), whose inverse images under the two projections of \(\widetilde{X}^{\mathrm{an}}\times_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\widetilde{X}^{\mathrm{an}}\) are the same. By IX 2, whose proof is valid in the analytic case, the morphism \(\widetilde{X}^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is a morphism of descent for the category of étale coverings, and consequently \(i\) comes from an isomorphism \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{X}'\).

b) Reduction to the case where \(X\) is regular. Let \(U\) be the open set of regular points of \(X\), \(i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), \(i^{\mathrm{an}}\colon U^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^{\mathrm{an}}\) the canonical morphisms; since \(X\) is normal, one has \(\operatorname{codim}(X-U,X)\geqslant 2\). Suppose that there exists an étale covering \(U'\) of \(U\) such that one has \(U^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{X}'|U^{\mathrm{an}}\) and let us show that then \(U'\) extends to an étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\) such that one has \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{X}'\). It suffices to see that \(U'\) extends to an étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\); indeed one will then have an isomorphism \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}|U^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{X}'|U^{\mathrm{an}}\); but, if \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) are the coherent sheaves of algebras on \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\) defining respectively \(\mathscr{X}'\) and \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\), the fact that \(X\) is normal and that one has \(\operatorname{codim}(X-U,X)\geqslant 2\) entails that the canonical morphisms

\[ \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*^{\mathrm{an}}(\mathscr{F}|U^{\mathrm{an}}) \qquad \mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*^{\mathrm{an}}(\mathscr{G}|U^{\mathrm{an}}) \]

are isomorphisms [11]. It follows that \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) hence also \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\) and \(\mathscr{X}'\) are isomorphic.

Let \(\varphi\colon X^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical morphism. Since the problem of extending \(U'\) to \(X\) is local on \(X\), it suffices to prove that, for every point \(y\) of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}- U^{\mathrm{an}}\), the étale covering \(U'_{\varphi(y)}=U'\times_{X}\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi(y)}\) of \(U_{\varphi(y)}=U\times_{X}\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi(y)}\) extends to \(\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi(y)}\). Let \(H\) be the coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{U}\)-Algebra defining \(U'\). The canonical morphism

\[ \alpha\colon (i_*H)^{\mathrm{an}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*^{\mathrm{an}}(H^{\mathrm{an}}) =\mathscr{F} \]

defines a morphism of sheaves of modules on \(\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},y}\):

\[ \alpha_{y}\colon (i_*H)^{\mathrm{an}}_{y} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}_{y}, \]

whose restriction to \(U_{y}=U_{\varphi(y)} \times_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi(y)}}\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},y}\) is an isomorphism. But this proves that \(H|U_{y}\) is trivial, hence that \(U'_{\varphi(y)}\) extends to \(\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi(y)}\).

c) Case where \(X\) is affine regular. Let

\[ j\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P \]

be a compactification of \(X\), where \(P\) is a projective \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme and \(j\) a dominant open immersion. Thanks to the resolution of singularities theorem [8], one can find a regular scheme \(R\), a projective morphism \(r\colon R\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P\), such that \(r\) induces an isomorphism \(r^{-1}(X)\simeq X\) and such that \(r^{-1}(X)\) is the complement in \(R\) of a normal crossings divisor. Let

\[ k\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R \]

be the canonical immersion. We are going to show that there exists a finite normal covering (0) \(\mathscr{R}'\) of \(R^{\mathrm{an}}\) that extends the étale covering \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\). By proposition 3 below, such a covering is unique; the problem of extending \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\) is therefore local on \(R^{\mathrm{an}}\) in a neighbourhood of \(R^{\mathrm{an}}- X^{\mathrm{an}}\). Now each point of \(R^{\mathrm{an}}- X^{\mathrm{an}}\) has an open neighbourhood \(\mathscr{V}\) isomorphic to a ball of an affine space \(\mathscr{E}^{n}\), such that \(\mathscr{V}- \mathscr{V}\cap X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is defined by the vanishing of the \(p\) first coordinate functions \(z_{1},\dots,z_{p}\), with \(0\leqslant p\leqslant n\). The fundamental group of \(\mathscr{U}=\mathscr{V}\cap X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Z}^{p}\), and every étale covering of \(\mathscr{U}\) is a quotient of a covering of the form

\[ \mathscr{U}''= \mathscr{U}[T_{1},\dots,T_{p}]/ (T_{1}^{n_{1}}-z_{1},\dots,T_{p}^{n_{p}}-z_{p}), \]

where the \(n_{i}\) are integers \(>0\), by a subgroup \(H\) of the Galois group \(\mathbf{Z}/n_{1}\mathbf{Z}\times\cdots\times\mathbf{Z}/n_{p}\mathbf{Z}\) of \(\mathscr{U}''\). Now \(\mathscr{U}''\) extends to the regular covering

\[ \mathscr{V}''= \mathscr{V}[T_{1},\dots,T_{p}]/ (T_{1}^{n_{1}}-z_{1},\dots,T_{p}^{n_{p}}-z_{p}), \]

of \(\mathscr{V}\) on which \(H\) operates, and the quotient of \(\mathscr{V}''\) by \(H\) is the sought extension.

The proof is then completed thanks to 6. The covering \(\mathscr{R}'\) comes from a finite covering \(R'\) of \(R\); the restriction of \(R'\) to \(X\) is a covering \(X'\) of \(X\) such that one has \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{X}'\), and according to 1(iii) \(X'\) is an étale covering of \(X\).

Corollary 2

Let \(X\) be a connected \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type, \(\varphi\colon X^{\mathrm{an}}{\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}} X\) the canonical morphism, \(x\) a point of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\). Let \(\pi_{1}(X^{\mathrm{an}},x)\) be the fundamental group of the topological space \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) at the point \(x\), \(\pi_{1}(X,\varphi(x))\) the fundamental group of the scheme \(X\) at the point \(\varphi(x)\) (V 7). Then \(\pi_{1}(X,\varphi(x))\) is canonically isomorphic to the completion of \(\pi_{1}(X^{\mathrm{an}},x)\), for the topology of subgroups of finite index.

Indeed, let \(\mathscr{C}\) be the category of finite étale coverings of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\), \(F\) the functor from \(\mathscr{C}\) into \(\mathbf{Ens}\) which, to every finite étale covering \(\mathscr{X}'\) of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\), associates the set of points of \(\mathscr{X}'\) above \(x\), and let \(\widehat{\pi}_{1}(X^{\mathrm{an}},x)\) be the profinite group associated with \(\mathscr{C}\) and \(F\) as stated in V 4. Since every finite étale covering of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is a quotient of the universal covering by a subgroup of finite index, \(\widehat{\pi}_{1}(X^{\mathrm{an}},x)\) is none other than the completion of \(\pi_{1}(X^{\mathrm{an}},x)\) for the topology of subgroups of finite index. The corollary thus follows from 1 and V 10.

Proposition 3

Let \(\mathscr{X}\) be a normal analytic space, \(\mathscr{Y}\) a closed analytic subset such that \(\mathscr{U}=\mathscr{X}-\mathscr{Y}\) is dense in \(\mathscr{X}\). Then the functor which, to every normal finite covering (0) \(\mathscr{X}'\) of \(\mathscr{X}\), associates its restriction to \(\mathscr{U}\) is fully faithful.

Let \(\mathscr{X}'\) and \(\mathscr{X}''\) be two normal finite coverings of \(\mathscr{X}\). One must show that the canonical map

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{X}}(\mathscr{X}',\mathscr{X}'') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{U}}(\mathscr{X}'|\mathscr{U},\mathscr{X}''|\mathscr{U}) \]

is bijective. Let \(u\), \(v\) be two \(\mathscr{X}\)-morphisms from \(\mathscr{X}'\) into \(\mathscr{X}''\) whose restrictions to \(\mathscr{U}\) are the same, and let us prove that \(u=v\). The morphisms \(u\) and \(v\) coincide on the dense open set \(\mathscr{U}\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{X}'\), hence on the underlying topological spaces. According to [4] this proves that one has \(u=v\).

Now let \(u\) be a \(\mathscr{U}\)-morphism from \(\mathscr{X}'|\mathscr{U}\) into \(\mathscr{X}''|\mathscr{U}\), and let us show that it extends to \(\mathscr{X}'\) in its entirety. One may assume \(\mathscr{X}'\) regular. Indeed, \(\mathscr{X}'\) being normal, one can find an open set \(\mathscr{V}\) of \(\mathscr{X}\) whose complement is an analytic part of codimension \(\geqslant 2\), such that \(\mathscr{X}'\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{V}=\mathscr{V}'\) is regular. Let \(\mathscr{V}''=\mathscr{X}''\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{V}\) and suppose the proposition proved for \(\mathscr{V}\). One considers the commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{\mathscr{V}'}\ar[dd]_{g'}\ar[rd]\ar[rrr]^{i'}&& &{\mathscr{X}'}\ar[dd]^{f'}\\ &{\mathscr{V}''}\ar[ld]_{g''\mkern-10mu}\ar[rrr]^-{i''~~}& & &{\mathscr{X}''}\ar[ld]_{f''\mkern-12mu}\\{\mathscr{V}}\ar[rrr]^{i}&& &{\mathscr{X}}\\ } \]

To \(u\) is associated a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}}\)-Algebras \(g''_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}'}\), from which one deduces a morphism

\[ i_*g''_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}''} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*g'_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}'}. \]

Taking into account the isomorphisms \(i'_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}'}\simeq\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'}\), \(i''_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}''}\simeq\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}''}\) [11] one deduces from this a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}}\)-Algebras

\[ f''_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}''} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'}, \]

whence the sought morphism \(\mathscr{X}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{X}''\).

One now assumes \(\mathscr{X}'\) regular. Let \(\mathscr{U}'=\mathscr{U}\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{X}'\), \(\mathscr{Y}'=\mathscr{X}'-\mathscr{U}'\). One considers \(\mathscr{Y}'\) as a reduced analytic subspace of \(\mathscr{X}'\); if \(\mathscr{Y}'_{1}\) is the singular closed set of \(\mathscr{Y}'\), one has \(\dim \mathscr{Y}'_{1} < \dim \mathscr{Y}'\) [4]. One thus sees, by induction on the dimension of \(\mathscr{Y}'\), that one may assume \(\mathscr{Y}'\) smooth. Since it suffices to extend \(u\) to an open neighbourhood of each point of \(\mathscr{Y}'\), one may assume, by the implicit function theorem, that \(\mathscr{X}'\) is a ball of an affine space \(\mathscr{E}^{n}\) and \(\mathscr{Y}'\) the closed set defined by the vanishing of the \(p\) first coordinate functions \(z_{1},\dots,z_{p}\), with \(0\leqslant p\leqslant n\).

To \(u\) one associates a section \(s\) of \(p\colon\mathscr{X}'\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{X}''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{X}'\) above \(\mathscr{U}'\); by restricting \(\mathscr{X}'\) if necessary, one may assume \(p_*(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{X}''})\) generated by elements \(x_{1},\dots,x_{q}\) of \(\Gamma(\mathscr{X}', p_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{X}''})\); let \(u_{1},\dots,u_{q}\in\Gamma(\mathscr{U}',\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'})\) be the images under \(s\) of \({x_{1}}|\mathscr{U}',\dots,{x_{q}}|\mathscr{U}'\). To say that \(s\) extends to \(\mathscr{X}'\) amounts to saying that the \(u_{1},\dots,u_{q}\) extend to sections of \(\Gamma(\mathscr{X}',\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'})\). But, since \(f\) is finite, each \(u_{i}\) is a Laurent series in \(z_{1},\dots,z_{p}\), with coefficients power series in \(z_{p{+}1},\dots,z_{n}\), which satisfy relations of integral dependence. It follows that \(u_{i}\) is bounded, hence is a power series in \(z_{1},\dots,z_{n}\), and consequently extends to \(\mathscr{X}'\).

One may ask whether the functor introduced in 3 is an equivalence of categories. One has an answer to this question thanks to the theorem of Grauert-Remmert [6] of which we give a proof below using the resolution of singularities. One could also have used the theorem of Grauert-Remmert to prove 1; this is what one did before having [8] at one's disposal.

.5cm

Theorem 4 (Theorem of Grauert-Remmert)

Let \(\mathscr{X}\) be a normal analytic space, \(\mathscr{Y}\) a closed analytic subset such that \(\mathscr{U}=\mathscr{X}-\mathscr{Y}\) is dense in \(\mathscr{X}\). Let \(\mathscr{U}'\) be a normal finite covering of \(\mathscr{U}\); suppose that there exists a rare closed analytic part \(\mathscr{S}\) of \(\mathscr{X}\) such that the restriction of \(\mathscr{U}'\) to \(\mathscr{U}-\mathscr{U}\cap\mathscr{S}\) is étale. Then there exists a normal finite covering \(\mathscr{X}'\) of \(\mathscr{X}\) which extends \(\mathscr{U}'\), and \(\mathscr{X}'\) is unique up to isomorphism.

Uniqueness follows from 3. The problem of extending \(\mathscr{U}'\) is therefore local on \(\mathscr{X}\). One may assume \(\mathscr{U}\) regular and \(\mathscr{U}'\) étale over \(\mathscr{U}\). Indeed the set of regular points of \(\mathscr{U}\) is an open set \(\mathscr{V}\) dense in \(\mathscr{X}\) whose complement is an analytic part [4] and it suffices to replace \(\mathscr{U}\) by the open set \(\mathscr{V}-\mathscr{V}\cap\mathscr{S}\).

Let \(y\) be a point of \(\mathscr{X}-\mathscr{U}\) and let us show that one can extend \(\mathscr{U}'\) to a neighbourhood of \(y\). Up to restricting \(\mathscr{X}\) to an open neighbourhood of \(y\), it follows from the theorem on resolution of singularities [8] that one can find a regular analytic space \(\mathscr{X}_1\), a projective morphism \(f\colon\mathscr{X}_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{X}\) inducing by restriction to \(\mathscr{U}\) an isomorphism \(\mathscr{U}_1=f^{-1}(\mathscr{U})\simeq\mathscr{U}\), such that \(\mathscr{U}_1\) is the complement in \(\mathscr{X}_1\) of a divisor with normal crossings. Let us show that \(\mathscr{U}'\) extends to a finite normal covering of \(\mathscr{X}_1\). Since the question is local on \(\mathscr{X}_1\), one may assume that \(\mathscr{X}_1\) is a ball in an affine space \(\mathscr{E}^n\) and that \(\mathscr{X}_1-\mathscr{U}_1\) is defined by the vanishing of the first \(p\) coordinate functions \(z_1,\dots,z_p\), with \(0\leqslant p\leqslant n\). The étale covering \(\mathscr{U}'\) of \(\mathscr{U}_1\) is a quotient of a covering of the form

\[ \mathscr{U}_2=\mathscr{U}_1[T_1,\dots,T_p]/\left(T_1^{n_1}-z_1,\dots, T_p^{n_p}-z_p\right) \]

by a subgroup \(H\) of the Galois group of \(\mathscr{U}_2\). The covering \(\mathscr{U}_2\) extends to the covering

\[ \mathscr{X}_2=\mathscr{X}_1[T_1,\dots,T_p]/\left(T_1^{n_1}-z_1,\dots, T_p^{n_p}-z_p\right) \]

of \(\mathscr{X}_1\) on which \(H\) acts, and \(\mathscr{X}_2/H\) extends \(\mathscr{U}'\) to \(\mathscr{X}_1\).

Let us denote by \(\mathscr{X}_1'\) the finite normal covering of \(\mathscr{X}_1\) which extends \(\mathscr{U}'\), by \(\mathscr{F}_1\) the coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}_1}\)-Algebra defined by \(\mathscr{F}_1\). By the finiteness theorem of Grauert-Remmert [4], \(f_*\mathscr{F}_1\) is a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}}\)-Algebra. There corresponds to it therefore a finite covering \(\mathscr{X}'\) of \(\mathscr{X}\) which is moreover normal since \(\mathscr{X}_1'\) is, and \(\mathscr{X}'\) is the sought-for extension of \(\mathscr{U}'\).

Remark 5

In statement 4, one cannot suppress the hypothesis on the locus of points where the morphism \(\mathscr{U}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{U}\) is not étale. Let for example \(\mathscr{X}\) be the unit disk of the complex plane, \(\mathscr{U}\) the complement of the origin in \(\mathscr{X}\), \(\mathscr{U}'=\mathscr{U}[T]/(T^2-\sin 1/z)\), where \(z\) is the coordinate function on \(\mathscr{X}\). Then \(\mathscr{U}'\) is a finite normal covering of \(\mathscr{U}\) which does not extend to \(\mathscr{X}\). Indeed, suppose that \(\mathscr{U}'\) extends to a finite covering \(\mathscr{X}'\) of \(\mathscr{X}\); the locus of points of \(\mathscr{X}\) where the morphism \(\mathscr{X}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{X}\) is not étale is then an analytic closed set which contains all the points \(z\) such that \(\sin 1/z=0\), which is absurd.

One can however suppress the hypothesis on the singular locus of the morphism \(\mathscr{U}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{U}\) when one has \(\operatorname{codim}(\mathscr{X}-\mathscr{U},\mathscr{X})\geqslant~2\). Indeed, one may assume \(\mathscr{U}\) regular. The locus of points of \(\mathscr{U}\) where \(\mathscr{U}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{U}\) is not étale is a divisor of \(\mathscr{U}\), and it follows from the theorem of Remmert-Stein [9] that it is the trace on \(\mathscr{U}\) of a divisor of \(\mathscr{X}\). Now, in this case, if \(\mathscr{A}\) is a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{U}}\)-Algebra such that \(\mathscr{U}'=\operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{an}(\mathscr{A})\), if \(i:\mathscr{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{X}\) is the canonical morphism, it suffices to take \(\mathscr{X}'=\operatorname{Spec}\mathrm{an}(i_*\mathscr{A})\); indeed, one knows that \(i_*\mathscr{A}\) is coherent [11].

Remark 0

There exist finitely presented groups \(G\), non trivial, which possess no subgroups of finite index, distinct from \(G\), for example the group of G. Higman (cf. J-P. Serre, Arbres et amalgames, Astérisque N\(^\circ\)46, prop. 6, chap. I, §1). Consequently, if such a group is realized as the topological fundamental group of a scheme \(V\) over \(\mathbf{C}\), say smooth and projective, the topological space \(V_{\mathrm{top}}\) underlying \(V\) is not simply connected, but \(V\) is algebraically simply connected. At present, no such \(V\) are known. Let us point out however that D. Toledo has constructed smooth and projective schemes \(V\) over \(\mathbf{C}\) whose topological fundamental group is not separated for the topology of subgroups of finite index (the natural morphism \(\pi_1(V_{\mathrm{top}})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(V_{\mathrm{alg}})\) is not injective). [D. Toledo, Projective varieties with non-residually finite fundamental group, Publ. Math. IHES 77 (1993), p. 103–119].

blocks.html: 5 Comparison theorems for étale coverings

Let us make precise the notion of a finite covering of an analytic space. If \(\mathscr{X}\) is an analytic space, one says that an analytic space \(\mathscr{X}'\) finite over \(\mathscr{X}\) is a finite covering of \(\mathscr{X}\) if every irreducible component of \(\mathscr{X}'\) dominates an irreducible component of \(\mathscr{X}\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1 (“Riemann Existence Theorem”)

Let \(X\) be a \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type, \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) the analytic space associated with \(X\). The functor \(\Psi\) which, to every finite étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\), associates \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\) is an equivalence of the category of finite étale coverings of \(X\) into the category of finite étale coverings of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\).
1) The functor \(\Psi\) is fully faithful. Let \(X'\) and \(X''\) be two finite étale coverings of \(X\), and let us prove that the canonical map

\[ \tag{*} { \operatorname{Hom}_{X}(X',X'') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}},X^{\prime\prime\mathrm{an}}) } \]

is bijective. One may assume \(X'\) connected. To give a \(X\)-morphism of \(X'\) into \(X''\) amounts to giving a connected component \(X_{i}\) of \(X'\times_{X}X''\) such that the morphism \(X_{i}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) induced by the first projection is an isomorphism. Since the connected components of \(X'\times_{X}X''\) correspond bijectively to the connected components of \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\times_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}X^{\prime\prime\mathrm{an}}\) (6) and since a morphism \(X_{i}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) is an isomorphism if and only if the same holds for \(X_{i}^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\), this proves the bijectivity of (*).

2) The functor \(\Psi\) is essentially surjective. Let \(\mathscr{X}'\) be a finite étale covering of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) and let us prove that there exists an étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\) such that one has an isomorphism \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathscr{X}'\). By 1) the question is local on \(X\), and one may therefore assume \(X\) affine.

a) Reduction to the case where \(X\) is normal. One may assume \(X\) reduced. Indeed, suppose the theorem proved for \(X_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\). The functor which, to a finite étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\) associates the finite étale covering \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) of \(X_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}^{\mathrm{an}}\) is then an equivalence. Since it is obtained by composing \(\Psi\) with the functor \(\Theta\) which, to a finite étale covering of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) associates its inverse image on \(X_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}^{\mathrm{an}}\), and since \(\Theta\) is fully faithful, this shows that \(\Psi\) is an equivalence of categories.

One may assume \(X\) normal. Indeed, let \(\widetilde{X}\) be the normalization of \(X\), \(p\colon\widetilde{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism. Since \(p\) is finite, \(p\) is a morphism of effective descent for the category of étale coverings (IX 7). The theorem being assumed proved for \(\widetilde{X}\), if one sets \(\widetilde{\mathscr{X}'}=\mathscr{X}'\times_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\widetilde{X}^{\mathrm{an}}\), there exists an étale covering \(\widetilde{X}'\) of \(\widetilde{X}\) and an isomorphism \(\widetilde{X}'{}^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq \widetilde{\mathscr{X}'}\). It then follows from 1) that the descent datum naturally borne by \(\widetilde{\mathscr{X}'}\) lifts to a descent datum on \(\widetilde{X}'\) relative to \(\widetilde{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\); this proves the existence of an étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\) such that one has an isomorphism \(i\colon X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\times_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\widetilde{X}^{\mathrm{an}} \simeq \widetilde{\mathscr{X}'}\), whose inverse images under the two projections of \(\widetilde{X}^{\mathrm{an}}\times_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\widetilde{X}^{\mathrm{an}}\) are the same. By IX 2, whose proof is valid in the analytic case, the morphism \(\widetilde{X}^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is a morphism of descent for the category of étale coverings, and consequently \(i\) comes from an isomorphism \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{X}'\).

b) Reduction to the case where \(X\) is regular. Let \(U\) be the open set of regular points of \(X\), \(i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), \(i^{\mathrm{an}}\colon U^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^{\mathrm{an}}\) the canonical morphisms; since \(X\) is normal, one has \(\operatorname{codim}(X-U,X)\geqslant 2\). Suppose that there exists an étale covering \(U'\) of \(U\) such that one has \(U^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{X}'|U^{\mathrm{an}}\) and let us show that then \(U'\) extends to an étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\) such that one has \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{X}'\). It suffices to see that \(U'\) extends to an étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\); indeed one will then have an isomorphism \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}|U^{\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{X}'|U^{\mathrm{an}}\); but, if \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) are the coherent sheaves of algebras on \(\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}}}\) defining respectively \(\mathscr{X}'\) and \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\), the fact that \(X\) is normal and that one has \(\operatorname{codim}(X-U,X)\geqslant 2\) entails that the canonical morphisms

\[ \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*^{\mathrm{an}}(\mathscr{F}|U^{\mathrm{an}}) \qquad \mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*^{\mathrm{an}}(\mathscr{G}|U^{\mathrm{an}}) \]
are isomorphisms [11]. It follows that \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) hence also \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\) and \(\mathscr{X}'\) are isomorphic.

Let \(\varphi\colon X^{\mathrm{an}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical morphism. Since the problem of extending \(U'\) to \(X\) is local on \(X\), it suffices to prove that, for every point \(y\) of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}- U^{\mathrm{an}}\), the étale covering \(U'_{\varphi(y)}=U'\times_{X}\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi(y)}\) of \(U_{\varphi(y)}=U\times_{X}\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi(y)}\) extends to \(\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi(y)}\). Let \(H\) be the coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{U}\)-Algebra defining \(U'\). The canonical morphism
\[ \alpha\colon (i_*H)^{\mathrm{an}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*^{\mathrm{an}}(H^{\mathrm{an}}) =\mathscr{F} \]
defines a morphism of sheaves of modules on \(\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},y}\):
\[ \alpha_{y}\colon (i_*H)^{\mathrm{an}}_{y} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}_{y}, \]

whose restriction to \(U_{y}=U_{\varphi(y)} \times_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi(y)}}\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X^{\mathrm{an}},y}\) is an isomorphism. But this proves that \(H|U_{y}\) is trivial, hence that \(U'_{\varphi(y)}\) extends to \(\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X,\varphi(y)}\).
c) Case where \(X\) is affine regular. Let
\[ j\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P \]

be a compactification of \(X\), where \(P\) is a projective \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme and \(j\) a dominant open immersion. Thanks to the resolution of singularities theorem [8], one can find a regular scheme \(R\), a projective morphism \(r\colon R\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P\), such that \(r\) induces an isomorphism \(r^{-1}(X)\simeq X\) and such that \(r^{-1}(X)\) is the complement in \(R\) of a normal crossings divisor. Let
\[ k\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R \]

be the canonical immersion. We are going to show that there exists a finite normal covering (0) \(\mathscr{R}'\) of \(R^{\mathrm{an}}\) that extends the étale covering \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\). By proposition 3 below, such a covering is unique; the problem of extending \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\) is therefore local on \(R^{\mathrm{an}}\) in a neighbourhood of \(R^{\mathrm{an}}- X^{\mathrm{an}}\). Now each point of \(R^{\mathrm{an}}- X^{\mathrm{an}}\) has an open neighbourhood \(\mathscr{V}\) isomorphic to a ball of an affine space \(\mathscr{E}^{n}\), such that \(\mathscr{V}- \mathscr{V}\cap X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is defined by the vanishing of the \(p\) first coordinate functions \(z_{1},\dots,z_{p}\), with \(0\leqslant p\leqslant n\). The fundamental group of \(\mathscr{U}=\mathscr{V}\cap X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Z}^{p}\), and every étale covering of \(\mathscr{U}\) is a quotient of a covering of the form
\[ \mathscr{U}''= \mathscr{U}[T_{1},\dots,T_{p}]/ (T_{1}^{n_{1}}-z_{1},\dots,T_{p}^{n_{p}}-z_{p}), \]

where the \(n_{i}\) are integers \(>0\), by a subgroup \(H\) of the Galois group \(\mathbf{Z}/n_{1}\mathbf{Z}\times\cdots\times\mathbf{Z}/n_{p}\mathbf{Z}\) of \(\mathscr{U}''\). Now \(\mathscr{U}''\) extends to the regular covering
\[ \mathscr{V}''= \mathscr{V}[T_{1},\dots,T_{p}]/ (T_{1}^{n_{1}}-z_{1},\dots,T_{p}^{n_{p}}-z_{p}), \]
of \(\mathscr{V}\) on which \(H\) operates, and the quotient of \(\mathscr{V}''\) by \(H\) is the sought extension.

The proof is then completed thanks to 6. The covering \(\mathscr{R}'\) comes from a finite covering \(R'\) of \(R\); the restriction of \(R'\) to \(X\) is a covering \(X'\) of \(X\) such that one has \(X^{\prime\mathrm{an}}\simeq \mathscr{X}'\), and according to 1(iii) \(X'\) is an étale covering of \(X\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Let \(X\) be a connected \(\mathbf{C}\)-scheme locally of finite type, \(\varphi\colon X^{\mathrm{an}}{\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}} X\) the canonical morphism, \(x\) a point of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\). Let \(\pi_{1}(X^{\mathrm{an}},x)\) be the fundamental group of the topological space \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) at the point \(x\), \(\pi_{1}(X,\varphi(x))\) the fundamental group of the scheme \(X\) at the point \(\varphi(x)\) (V 7). Then \(\pi_{1}(X,\varphi(x))\) is canonically isomorphic to the completion of \(\pi_{1}(X^{\mathrm{an}},x)\), for the topology of subgroups of finite index.

Indeed, let \(\mathscr{C}\) be the category of finite étale coverings of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\), \(F\) the functor from \(\mathscr{C}\) into \(\mathbf{Ens}\) which, to every finite étale covering \(\mathscr{X}'\) of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\), associates the set of points of \(\mathscr{X}'\) above \(x\), and let \(\widehat{\pi}_{1}(X^{\mathrm{an}},x)\) be the profinite group associated with \(\mathscr{C}\) and \(F\) as stated in V 4. Since every finite étale covering of \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is a quotient of the universal covering by a subgroup of finite index, \(\widehat{\pi}_{1}(X^{\mathrm{an}},x)\) is none other than the completion of \(\pi_{1}(X^{\mathrm{an}},x)\) for the topology of subgroups of finite index. The corollary thus follows from 1 and V 10.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3

Let \(\mathscr{X}\) be a normal analytic space, \(\mathscr{Y}\) a closed analytic subset such that \(\mathscr{U}=\mathscr{X}-\mathscr{Y}\) is dense in \(\mathscr{X}\). Then the functor which, to every normal finite covering (0) \(\mathscr{X}'\) of \(\mathscr{X}\), associates its restriction to \(\mathscr{U}\) is fully faithful.
Let \(\mathscr{X}'\) and \(\mathscr{X}''\) be two normal finite coverings of \(\mathscr{X}\). One must show that the canonical map

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{X}}(\mathscr{X}',\mathscr{X}'') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{U}}(\mathscr{X}'|\mathscr{U},\mathscr{X}''|\mathscr{U}) \]

is bijective. Let \(u\), \(v\) be two \(\mathscr{X}\)-morphisms from \(\mathscr{X}'\) into \(\mathscr{X}''\) whose restrictions to \(\mathscr{U}\) are the same, and let us prove that \(u=v\). The morphisms \(u\) and \(v\) coincide on the dense open set \(\mathscr{U}\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{X}'\), hence on the underlying topological spaces. According to [4] this proves that one has \(u=v\).

Now let \(u\) be a \(\mathscr{U}\)-morphism from \(\mathscr{X}'|\mathscr{U}\) into \(\mathscr{X}''|\mathscr{U}\), and let us show that it extends to \(\mathscr{X}'\) in its entirety. One may assume \(\mathscr{X}'\) regular. Indeed, \(\mathscr{X}'\) being normal, one can find an open set \(\mathscr{V}\) of \(\mathscr{X}\) whose complement is an analytic part of codimension \(\geqslant 2\), such that \(\mathscr{X}'\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{V}=\mathscr{V}'\) is regular. Let \(\mathscr{V}''=\mathscr{X}''\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{V}\) and suppose the proposition proved for \(\mathscr{V}\). One considers the commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{\mathscr{V}'}\ar[dd]_{g'}\ar[rd]\ar[rrr]^{i'}&& &{\mathscr{X}'}\ar[dd]^{f'}\\ &{\mathscr{V}''}\ar[ld]_{g''\mkern-10mu}\ar[rrr]^-{i''~~}& & &{\mathscr{X}''}\ar[ld]_{f''\mkern-12mu}\\{\mathscr{V}}\ar[rrr]^{i}&& &{\mathscr{X}}\\ } \]

To \(u\) is associated a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}}\)-Algebras \(g''_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}'}\), from which one deduces a morphism
\[ i_*g''_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}''} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*g'_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}'}. \]

Taking into account the isomorphisms \(i'_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}'}\simeq\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'}\), \(i''_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{V}''}\simeq\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}''}\) [11] one deduces from this a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}}\)-Algebras
\[ f''_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}''} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'}, \]
whence the sought morphism \(\mathscr{X}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{X}''\).

One now assumes \(\mathscr{X}'\) regular. Let \(\mathscr{U}'=\mathscr{U}\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{X}'\), \(\mathscr{Y}'=\mathscr{X}'-\mathscr{U}'\). One considers \(\mathscr{Y}'\) as a reduced analytic subspace of \(\mathscr{X}'\); if \(\mathscr{Y}'_{1}\) is the singular closed set of \(\mathscr{Y}'\), one has \(\dim \mathscr{Y}'_{1} < \dim \mathscr{Y}'\) [4]. One thus sees, by induction on the dimension of \(\mathscr{Y}'\), that one may assume \(\mathscr{Y}'\) smooth. Since it suffices to extend \(u\) to an open neighbourhood of each point of \(\mathscr{Y}'\), one may assume, by the implicit function theorem, that \(\mathscr{X}'\) is a ball of an affine space \(\mathscr{E}^{n}\) and \(\mathscr{Y}'\) the closed set defined by the vanishing of the \(p\) first coordinate functions \(z_{1},\dots,z_{p}\), with \(0\leqslant p\leqslant n\).

To \(u\) one associates a section \(s\) of \(p\colon\mathscr{X}'\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{X}''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{X}'\) above \(\mathscr{U}'\); by restricting \(\mathscr{X}'\) if necessary, one may assume \(p_*(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{X}''})\) generated by elements \(x_{1},\dots,x_{q}\) of \(\Gamma(\mathscr{X}', p_*\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'\times_{\mathscr{X}}\mathscr{X}''})\); let \(u_{1},\dots,u_{q}\in\Gamma(\mathscr{U}',\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'})\) be the images under \(s\) of \({x_{1}}|\mathscr{U}',\dots,{x_{q}}|\mathscr{U}'\). To say that \(s\) extends to \(\mathscr{X}'\) amounts to saying that the \(u_{1},\dots,u_{q}\) extend to sections of \(\Gamma(\mathscr{X}',\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}'})\). But, since \(f\) is finite, each \(u_{i}\) is a Laurent series in \(z_{1},\dots,z_{p}\), with coefficients power series in \(z_{p{+}1},\dots,z_{n}\), which satisfy relations of integral dependence. It follows that \(u_{i}\) is bounded, hence is a power series in \(z_{1},\dots,z_{n}\), and consequently extends to \(\mathscr{X}'\).

One may ask whether the functor introduced in 3 is an equivalence of categories. One has an answer to this question thanks to the theorem of Grauert-Remmert [6] of which we give a proof below using the resolution of singularities. One could also have used the theorem of Grauert-Remmert to prove 1; this is what one did before having [8] at one's disposal.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4 (Theorem of Grauert-Remmert)

Let \(\mathscr{X}\) be a normal analytic space, \(\mathscr{Y}\) a closed analytic subset such that \(\mathscr{U}=\mathscr{X}-\mathscr{Y}\) is dense in \(\mathscr{X}\). Let \(\mathscr{U}'\) be a normal finite covering of \(\mathscr{U}\); suppose that there exists a rare closed analytic part \(\mathscr{S}\) of \(\mathscr{X}\) such that the restriction of \(\mathscr{U}'\) to \(\mathscr{U}-\mathscr{U}\cap\mathscr{S}\) is étale. Then there exists a normal finite covering \(\mathscr{X}'\) of \(\mathscr{X}\) which extends \(\mathscr{U}'\), and \(\mathscr{X}'\) is unique up to isomorphism.

Uniqueness follows from 3. The problem of extending \(\mathscr{U}'\) is therefore local on \(\mathscr{X}\). One may assume \(\mathscr{U}\) regular and \(\mathscr{U}'\) étale over \(\mathscr{U}\). Indeed the set of regular points of \(\mathscr{U}\) is an open set \(\mathscr{V}\) dense in \(\mathscr{X}\) whose complement is an analytic part [4] and it suffices to replace \(\mathscr{U}\) by the open set \(\mathscr{V}-\mathscr{V}\cap\mathscr{S}\).

Let \(y\) be a point of \(\mathscr{X}-\mathscr{U}\) and let us show that one can extend \(\mathscr{U}'\) to a neighbourhood of \(y\). Up to restricting \(\mathscr{X}\) to an open neighbourhood of \(y\), it follows from the theorem on resolution of singularities [8] that one can find a regular analytic space \(\mathscr{X}_1\), a projective morphism \(f\colon\mathscr{X}_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{X}\) inducing by restriction to \(\mathscr{U}\) an isomorphism \(\mathscr{U}_1=f^{-1}(\mathscr{U})\simeq\mathscr{U}\), such that \(\mathscr{U}_1\) is the complement in \(\mathscr{X}_1\) of a divisor with normal crossings. Let us show that \(\mathscr{U}'\) extends to a finite normal covering of \(\mathscr{X}_1\). Since the question is local on \(\mathscr{X}_1\), one may assume that \(\mathscr{X}_1\) is a ball in an affine space \(\mathscr{E}^n\) and that \(\mathscr{X}_1-\mathscr{U}_1\) is defined by the vanishing of the first \(p\) coordinate functions \(z_1,\dots,z_p\), with \(0\leqslant p\leqslant n\). The étale covering \(\mathscr{U}'\) of \(\mathscr{U}_1\) is a quotient of a covering of the form
\[ \mathscr{U}_2=\mathscr{U}_1[T_1,\dots,T_p]/\left(T_1^{n_1}-z_1,\dots, T_p^{n_p}-z_p\right) \]
by a subgroup \(H\) of the Galois group of \(\mathscr{U}_2\). The covering \(\mathscr{U}_2\) extends to the covering
\[ \mathscr{X}_2=\mathscr{X}_1[T_1,\dots,T_p]/\left(T_1^{n_1}-z_1,\dots, T_p^{n_p}-z_p\right) \]
of \(\mathscr{X}_1\) on which \(H\) acts, and \(\mathscr{X}_2/H\) extends \(\mathscr{U}'\) to \(\mathscr{X}_1\).

Let us denote by \(\mathscr{X}_1'\) the finite normal covering of \(\mathscr{X}_1\) which extends \(\mathscr{U}'\), by \(\mathscr{F}_1\) the coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}_1}\)-Algebra defined by \(\mathscr{F}_1\). By the finiteness theorem of Grauert-Remmert [4], \(f_*\mathscr{F}_1\) is a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{X}}\)-Algebra. There corresponds to it therefore a finite covering \(\mathscr{X}'\) of \(\mathscr{X}\) which is moreover normal since \(\mathscr{X}_1'\) is, and \(\mathscr{X}'\) is the sought-for extension of \(\mathscr{U}'\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5

In statement 4, one cannot suppress the hypothesis on the locus of points where the morphism \(\mathscr{U}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{U}\) is not étale. Let for example \(\mathscr{X}\) be the unit disk of the complex plane, \(\mathscr{U}\) the complement of the origin in \(\mathscr{X}\), \(\mathscr{U}'=\mathscr{U}[T]/(T^2-\sin 1/z)\), where \(z\) is the coordinate function on \(\mathscr{X}\). Then \(\mathscr{U}'\) is a finite normal covering of \(\mathscr{U}\) which does not extend to \(\mathscr{X}\). Indeed, suppose that \(\mathscr{U}'\) extends to a finite covering \(\mathscr{X}'\) of \(\mathscr{X}\); the locus of points of \(\mathscr{X}\) where the morphism \(\mathscr{X}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{X}\) is not étale is then an analytic closed set which contains all the points \(z\) such that \(\sin 1/z=0\), which is absurd.

One can however suppress the hypothesis on the singular locus of the morphism \(\mathscr{U}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{U}\) when one has \(\operatorname{codim}(\mathscr{X}-\mathscr{U},\mathscr{X})\geqslant~2\). Indeed, one may assume \(\mathscr{U}\) regular. The locus of points of \(\mathscr{U}\) where \(\mathscr{U}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{U}\) is not étale is a divisor of \(\mathscr{U}\), and it follows from the theorem of Remmert-Stein [9] that it is the trace on \(\mathscr{U}\) of a divisor of \(\mathscr{X}\). Now, in this case, if \(\mathscr{A}\) is a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{U}}\)-Algebra such that \(\mathscr{U}'=\operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{an}(\mathscr{A})\), if \(i:\mathscr{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{X}\) is the canonical morphism, it suffices to take \(\mathscr{X}'=\operatorname{Spec}\mathrm{an}(i_*\mathscr{A})\); indeed, one knows that \(i_*\mathscr{A}\) is coherent [11].

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 0

There exist finitely presented groups \(G\), non trivial, which possess no subgroups of finite index, distinct from \(G\), for example the group of G. Higman (cf. J-P. Serre, Arbres et amalgames, Astérisque N\(^\circ\)46, prop. 6, chap. I, §1). Consequently, if such a group is realized as the topological fundamental group of a scheme \(V\) over \(\mathbf{C}\), say smooth and projective, the topological space \(V_{\mathrm{top}}\) underlying \(V\) is not simply connected, but \(V\) is algebraically simply connected. At present, no such \(V\) are known. Let us point out however that D. Toledo has constructed smooth and projective schemes \(V\) over \(\mathbf{C}\) whose topological fundamental group is not separated for the topology of subgroups of finite index (the natural morphism \(\pi_1(V_{\mathrm{top}})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(V_{\mathrm{alg}})\) is not injective). [D. Toledo, Projective varieties with non-residually finite fundamental group, Publ. Math. IHES 77 (1993), p. 103–119].
